text: ### Buried Woes Continued Volume One Liu Ting Liu Shao Bao Ting, courtesy name Xingwu. As a general, he lived at home. At that time, bandits suddenly rose up in Ningzhou, their power greatly expanded. The provincial governor sent the prefect and the governor to request help, but Liu Shao Bao refused on the grounds of illness. The governor then sent other officials to persuade him, but he still refused. All of them were very angry. They ordered a doctor to go and verify his condition; if he was faking, they would report him. When the doctor arrived, Liu Shao Bao was lying weakly in bed. The officials were anxious and had no solution. Soon after, they suddenly received a report that General Liu had defeated the bandits and returned. They were greatly startled and said, "We couldn't have known the general would go, so why did he use such cunning?" Liu Shao Bao replied, "The bandits are descendants of Chen Youliang, who had been plotting for generations to seize power. If they had known I would come, they would have been well-prepared, so I attacked them secretly. This is a military strategy." Everyone then understood. At that time, there was a strong emphasis on literature, and during official gatherings, Liu Shao Bao was seated below the scholars. During a public banquet organized by the local gentry, the funds were insufficient. They would call out to Liu Shao Bao by his courtesy name, saying, "Xingwu, you handle this." Liu Shao Bao always contributed ten or a hundred times more than the others. Sometimes, when he was drunk, he would order his servants to ride horses to entertain the guests. When Liu Shao Bao got excited, he would often get on his horse and dance with two swords. The spectators only saw white mist swirling and blinding their eyes, unable to see his face. Although they were amazed by his skill, they also treated it as a form of entertainment.

The Son of the Chief Minister, Niandu, was agile and possessed his father's style. However, the Chief Minister's concealed crossbow was a supreme skill, capable of penetrating tough armor at a distance of fifty to sixty steps; Niandu could only reach about twenty steps and could not penetrate the armor, thus falling short in bravery. The Chief Minister also had a daughter who was brave. She married into a certain family, and her dowry was abundant. One day, several dozen bandits broke through the defenses of their home, causing the entire family to panic. The daughter ordered her maid to take soft armor and put it on, then led the maids to draw their swords and go out to kill the bandits. The bandits could not withstand the attack and fled away. According to the *History of Ming*, the entry for the Chief Minister details his campaigns against the Mien, Luo Xiong, Bo Chiu, Japanese, and Lolo, which are recorded in great detail. However, it omits the campaign to pacify Ningzhou, because the bandits were quickly defeated. Nonetheless, his achievements in devising ingenious strategies were significant. As for the contemporary society's disdain for martial valor, if there were high-ranking officials in the neighborhood who married into the Chief Minister's family, they would be stripped of their official status. This explains the decline of the Ming dynasty. According to: The Chief Minister was particularly skilled in *bájù* (a form of wrestling), able to leap ten meters vertically and horizontally. *Bájù* is mentioned in the *Zuo Zhuan* as "Wei Chou leaped three hundred times and jumped three hundred times," and in the *Book of Han* as "Gan Yanshou, a young man from a good family, served as a guard in the imperial army, skilled in archery, throwing stones, and *bájù*, and once leaped over the guard tower of the imperial army."

Further examination reveals that this essay is found in Zhang Guatian's collection. The original text at the end mentions, "The *Ming Shi* lists the achievements of the Shao Bao in pacifying Min, Luo Xiong, Bo Chiu, the Japanese, Korea, and the Luos," which seems to be in error. It is likely that the Shao Bao, when pacifying the Japanese, actually collaborated with Korean forces. Therefore, I have specifically removed these three characters. Huang Zhen During the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, Yu Zhongcheng and Tan Youxia formed a literary society in Jinling. Huang Gong, known as Shi Zhai, happened to visit, and they established a friendship. The two were quite compatible. Huang Gong always adhered to propriety, even in haste, and the others admired him but found him somewhat rigid. They thought of testing him. There was a courtesan named Gu, who was renowned for her beauty and intelligence, and well-versed in history and literature. When she played the zither and sang, those who saw her were captivated. One day, it rained heavily, and they held a banquet for Huang Gong in Yu's garden. They invited Gu to join in the drinking, and Huang Gong showed no displeasure. The others took turns toasting him, and they drank heavily, becoming quite drunk. After sending Huang Gong to bed, they specially set up a small bed with pillows, quilts, and mats. They instructed Gu to remove her undergarments. They locked the door and waited outside. Huang Gong woke up in a panic, unable to find his clothes, and covered himself with the quilt. He then told Gu to use the mat for sleeping. The mat was thick and narrow, making it difficult to turn over, so he instructed her to use the quilt instead. Gu then came close to Huang Gong, and he calmly said, "It is unnecessary." He turned his body inward, took a few deep breaths, and fell asleep. By the fourth drum of the night, he woke up and turned his face outward. Gu pretended to be asleep and lay beside him, while Huang Gong continued to sleep soundly. The next morning, Gu left and described the events in detail. She also said, "You all are famous scholars, composing poetry and drinking wine, enjoying these pleasures. To become a saint or a Buddha, to achieve loyalty and filial piety, ultimately belongs to Mr. Huang."

When the Ming dynasty fell, he was captured in Jinling. In prison, he recited the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) and the *Zhouyi* (Book of Changes) daily. After several days, his appearance became more robust. On the eve of his execution, an old prison guard held needles and thread and wept, saying, "This is the final service I can offer to my master!" He replied, "I die properly, which is to die in accordance with propriety. Why are you sad?" Old friends brought wine and meat to bid him farewell, and he ate and drank as usual. He slept soundly until dawn, then washed and changed clothes. He told his servant, "Earlier, someone asked me to write something on a scroll. I had already agreed, so I cannot let them down." He mixed ink, spread out the paper, first writing in small regular script, then in running script. The scroll was very long, so he finished it in large characters. He stamped his seal and then went out to face execution. The scroll was later kept by someone in Jinling. After meeting him, the Gu family blamed him for returning to a certain official post. When Li Zicheng captured Beijing, the Gu family thought that since their husband could die, they would hang themselves first. But he could not use them.

The Historian said: This is a record of the extraordinary deeds of Mr. Huang, as documented in *The Wangxi Anthology*, alongside the story of Left Zhongyi Gong. From ancient times, loyal ministers and righteous men have always taken the world as their responsibility. Even in times of chaos and transformation, they would still strive to stabilize the nation with their own strength, and if necessary, sacrifice their lives for the country, believing that they could not bear the blame for their duties. Therefore, when they calmly accepted their fate, even the names of loyalty and righteousness were not spoken, and what concern was there for their families or personal affairs, let alone the temptations of pleasure, wealth, and material gain? I have read Buddhist texts, and Kasyapa said, "The body of a Buddha is indestructible by the fires of the world." The *Yogacara* school also states, "Mara has four daughters, who are extremely beautiful. They came before the Bodhisattva, displaying various seductive poses. The Bodhisattva, with his moral strength and mental discipline, transformed the four daughters into old and ugly women, causing them to retreat in shame." This shows that reason cannot overcome desire, which can even make the most courageous and intelligent people lose their virtue and wisdom, but only immortals and Buddhas can control it. However, while immortals and Buddhas are completely unmoved, sages and virtuous people can be both active and passive. In the case of Left Gong, who suffered such a severe misfortune, his few solemn words still resonate with vitality today. If he had always been as refined and cultured as the Gu family, and if he had lived in close intimacy with them, how could it have moved him even slightly? This is the intention of the historian to uncover hidden truths and clarify subtle meanings. As for Mr. Huang's final words, they were determined by fate, just as with the two individuals. But was this their original intention? This is something that can make one weep for the two individuals.

**Suicide by Hanging** From *As I Have Heard*: In the capital city, there was a wealthy family named Lu who had recently married off their daughter. The couple, both handsome and graceful, were admired by all as if they were gods or immortals. Observing their expressions, it was evident that they were very fond of each other. The next morning, the door remained closed. Peering through the window, it was seen that they had hung themselves, one on the left and one on the right. Looking at their bed, they were still in their wedding clothes, having not separated before their suicide. The female servants and maids all said, "They had already undressed the previous day, why did they dress in their finest clothes before dying?" This case, even the legendary judge Gaotao could not comprehend.

According to the *Zhongxing Jueyao Ci Xuan* edited by Hua An: Wu Li San of Qiantang, courtesy name Zihe, had a five-volume collection of lyrics titled *Shunshou Lao Ren Ci*. There was a Tao Shi who had a deep emotional bond with Wang, a young man. Although they met briefly like floating萍 (萍水) in water during their lifetimes, they would inevitably be permanently separated after death. Thus, they drowned themselves in West Lake during the moonlit night. Wu Li San composed the lyric *Shuang Tian Xiaojiao* to mourn their deaths, which reads: "The linked rings are easily broken, but the heart-knot is hard to untie. Foolish yet talented couple, their emotional bond was strong, and they feared separation. Their feelings were deep, and their path to reunion was cut off; together they sank into the vast misty waters. Where do their wandering spirits wander? The moon over the long bridge, the moon over the short bridge." This story is also recorded in the *Xihu Zhi* (Treatise on West Lake). Indeed, there are such passionate lovers in the world. After the Lu Shi couple's reunion, could they not also have died for love? Living Sacrifice In the 15th year of the Chongzhen era (1642), Hong Chengchou was defeated by the Qing dynasty, and it was rumored that he had died a martyr. The Chongzhen emperor granted sixteen altars for the sacrifice and personally wrote an eulogy to honor him. Later, when the Qing army advanced into the Jiangnan region, Hong Chengchou was appointed to govern the Jiangnan, Sichuan, and Hubei provinces. After entering the pass, a local person welcomed and requested an audience with Hong Chengchou, who accepted the request. The local person knelt respectfully upon entering and took out the emperor's eulogy, reciting it aloud before weeping and leaving.

In assessing Hong Chengchou's abilities, it seems he was still capable of handling military affairs among the officials of late Ming. The recorded achievements in the historical records are also noteworthy. However, Huang Zongxi once criticized the exaggeration of these recorded achievements, suggesting that they are not entirely reliable. According to *Yanpo Zaji* (A Miscellany of Exposure), when Hong Chengchou suffered a defeat, his family members at home had already engraved his biography and distributed it to mourners. At the same time, Emperor Chongzhen was conducting sacrifices at the 14 altars, and news of Hong Chengchou's surrender reached the court. Later, Jin Sheng led a rebellion in Huizhou, and his disciple Jiang Tianyi also joined him in defeat. Hong Chengchou ordered them to surrender, and Jiang Tianyi recited the imperial eulogy to shame him. Afterward, when Hong Chengchou returned to the Qing court and passed away in Beijing, his family members engraved another biography, no longer mentioning his previous loyalty to the Ming dynasty, starting instead from his entry into the Qing court. A frivolous person obtained both biographies, compiled them into one book, and used it as a source of amusement. The *History of Ming* also states: In the 14th year of the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, the Qing forces surrounded Songshan. Hong Chengchou and Qiu Minyang swore to die in defense, but no external aid arrived, and their supplies of fodder and grain were exhausted. By the second month of the following year, they had already been besieged for half a year. Unable to hold out any longer, the city fell, and Hong Chengchou surrendered. Qiu Minyang refused to surrender and died. He was posthumously granted the title of Right Vice Minister of Judicial Inspection, received the imperial sacrifice at six altars, and the government arranged for his burial. The emperor ordered the construction of a temple in the capital, placing him alongside Hong Chengchou. The emperor intended to personally attend the sacrifice, but upon hearing of Hong Chengchou's surrender, he decided against it. Anecdotes about Xiong Xiangmin

The *Quan Xie Shan Ji* records: A person from Shining named Ni told me that his father had once seen a book titled *Qiu Cao Ri Lu* from his ancestors' lineage while visiting the Tancheng family in the village of Cangqiao. This person served as a prison official during the reign of Emperor Xizong. The book documented the cases of virtuous individuals killed by Wei Zhongxian, who were not sent to the Eastern Factory but rather to the Ministry of Justice. Ni recounted that Xuanye, upon entering prison, had every drink and meal reported to Wei Zhongxian by the prison officials. However, Xuanye showed no unusual reaction. He always slept with a rattan pillow, regardless of the season, and never left it. Every night when the surroundings were quiet, he would perform a ritual of bowing twice to the North Star, offering the rattan pillow to it, though no one could understand the meaning behind this. When asked about it, Xuanye merely smiled and did not respond. Eventually, the day of his execution arrived, and Xuanye remained calm, presenting a final memorial to the emperor, still discussing border affairs, and composing a farewell poem. The draft of his memorial was intercepted by the officials of the Ministry of Justice, who said, "How can a prisoner submit a memorial?" Xuanye replied, "This is Zhao Gao's words." (Note: Twelve characters are missing here.) "How could the holy court allow such a thing?" He then calmly accepted his fate. At the time, there was an order to send his head to the nine border regions. When the officials of the Ministry of Justice recorded his execution, they found the execution ground empty, with only the rattan pillow remaining. They were greatly alarmed and warned each other not to reveal what had happened. They secretly reported to Wei Zhongxian, who ordered the interrogation of members of the Xiong family. After a thorough search, they found nothing. Wei Zhongxian, having no other options, kept the matter secret. The heads sent to the nine border regions were not the real ones. After Wei Zhongxian's downfall, Prince Zhao Bi submitted multiple memorials requesting the return of Xuanye's head to be buried in Puzhou, knowing full well that it was not the real head, but using it to resolve this unjust case.

This account is not mentioned in the unofficial histories of the Ming dynasty. I believe that Li Gong Yingbi's *Sanyuan Jizhi* (Notes of the Three Palaces) describes the execution of Xiang Min in great detail, which contradicts the current account. However, Chen, who personally witnessed the event, is likely telling the truth. (Chen is the Grand Secretary of Pu Prefecture, Han Kuan.) According to historical records, the draft only records Xiang Min's achievements in protecting Liaodong but does not mention his knowledge of divination. Only during his inspection of Jinzhou in the 35th year of Wanli's reign, when there was a severe drought, did he pray to the city god, threatening to destroy the temple if it did not rain within three days. Upon reaching Guangning, after three days without rain, he wrote a white notice, sealed his sword, and sent envoys to destroy the temple. Before the envoys arrived, a fierce wind and thunderstorm broke out, and it rained heavily. The people of Liaodong believed the city god to be a deity. Based on this, the events in the prison are not baseless. **Earthquake** *A Brief Account of Heavenly Changes*: On the sixth day of the fifth month, the temple priest at the Hada Gate Fire God Temple saw the fire god moving and about to descend from the altar. He quickly lit incense and knelt to plead, saying, "Lord Fire God, it is very early outside, please do not move." The fire god lifted his foot to leave, and the temple priest cried and tried to hold him back. As they were struggling, the earthquake began. There was a man from Shaoxing who was the younger brother of a respected official. After arriving in the capital for three days, he bought a blue silk robe from Cai Market and returned, swaying as he walked. On the way, he met six people. While bowing and greeting, his head suddenly flew off and embedded itself into the wall. His eyes flew onto the opposite wall, still moving. His eyebrows also stuck together. The six people he met were unharmed.

At the intersection of the Guangdong Guild Hall, a tutor opened a school with thirty-two young boys. After a loud sound, both the teacher and the students disappeared. In another incident, a newly appointed general in Xuanfu was walking along Yuanhong Temple Street when a loud sound caused him and his seven attendants to vanish. The injured men and women were all naked, not a thread on their bodies, and no one knew why. One attendant, struck by lightning, lost his hat, clothes, trousers, shoes, and socks in an instant. Another person, injured and lying on the ground, saw a naked woman pass by. Some covered their private parts with tiles, others with half a garter, half a blanket, or a sheet, and in a moment, dozens of people appeared. This person was both in pain and laughing. On the street near Yuanhong Temple, a sedan chair passed by, and a loud sound lifted the top off. The woman inside was completely naked, but she remained unharmed. Only the wife of a certain official was running naked in the middle of the street, which was a shocking sight. Along the Chang'an Street, heads were falling from the sky, some with eyebrows and noses, others with foreheads, in a flurry. A large piece of wood flew to the Stone Marquis Street in Miyun, and a five-thousand-pound stone lion flew out of the Shuncheng Gate. After the earthquake, someone reported that clothes had been blown to the West Mountains and hung on tree branches. In the training ground of Changping County, clothes were piled up, and household items, money, and jewelry were also present. In front of the De Sheng Gate, there were many more heads and arms, indicating a particularly severe impact.

Prior to this, on the first day of the fifth month, the magistrate of Jinan, Shandong, went to the City God Temple to burn incense. Upon reaching the temple gate, suddenly all the magistrate's attendants fell into a trance. One attendant's wife came to see her husband and saw that her former husband, who had died many years ago, was serving in the temple. The deceased husband said, "One cannot enter the temple, as all the city gods are here registering." According to the *Record of Strange Events*: During the reign of Emperor Shenzong of Song in the year 1074, in Wucheng County of En Prefecture, a whirlwind came from the southwest, uprooting houses and trees. The county magistrate's family and the people were all sucked up into the sky, and those who fell to the ground and died were countless. In the year 1830 during the reign of Emperor Daoguang, the area of Zhili was split open for no less than a thousand li, and those who were crushed to death were counted in the tens of thousands, but there were none who had their clothes and limbs blown away. However, this record states it so firmly. Examining the *Ming Shi* (History of the Ming Dynasty) and the *Wu Xing Zhi* (Treatise on the Five Elements), there is no record of a disaster on the sixth day of the fifth month. But the *Ming Shi* has many omissions and errors. For example, in the reign of Emperor Tianqi, on the night of the fifth day of the third month in the fourth year and on the night of the sixth day of the sixth month, there were earthquakes in the capital, which are recorded in both the *Emperor's Biography* and the *Wu Xing Zhi*. Only the *Wu Xing Zhi* mentions three earthquakes in the capital in the third year, which the *Emperor's Biography* does not record; the *Emperor's Biography* mentions another earthquake in the capital on the night of the fifth day of the third month in the fourth year, which the *Wu Xing Zhi* does not record. On the night of the day of the Earthly Branch Geng Shen, there were three more earthquakes, but the *Wu Xing Zhi* only mentions a second earthquake. Thus, the *Ming Shi* is not entirely reliable.

Mr. Qiuquan, a renowned doctor in our county, was once approached by a wealthy man who was gravely ill and on the brink of death. The man invited Mr. Qiuquan to treat him, but at the time, Mr. Qiuquan was attending to a nobleman's illness and was unable to come. The wealthy man, filled with anxiety, remained in a weakened state throughout the night and told his son, "If only I could see Mr. Qiuquan once, I would die without regret." The son then repeatedly went to Mr. Qiuquan's residence, kneeling and bowing to urge him to come. Just as the nobleman's condition had greatly improved, and several days later, the nobleman had recovered, Mr. Qiuquan was invited to a feast where gold and coins were offered to him. Mr. Qiuquan drank heavily and returned to his boat, where he told his family, "I can now fulfill the wealthy man's request." Meanwhile, the servant sent by the wealthy man's son was eagerly untying the boat and rowing towards the wealthy man's home. Upon arrival, the servant announced, "Mr. Qiuquan has arrived!" The entire household was surprised and welcomed him with great joy. Mr. Qiuquan was still in a deep sleep when his family woke him up. The wealthy man, dressed formally, bowed deeply and entered the boat to welcome him. Mr. Qiuquan apologized for the late hour and asked to come the next morning after washing and grooming. The wealthy man insisted, saying, "My father has been waiting for you, enduring great pain. Please come now; why bother with washing and grooming?" He forced Mr. Qiuquan to enter. After diagnosing the pulse and giving medicine, Mr. Qiuquan left. The wealthy man offered a feast, but Mr. Qiuquan merely waved his hand in refusal. He returned to the boat, undressed, and lay down to sleep. When the rooster crowed and he woke up from his drunkenness, Mr. Qiuquan called out to his family and scolded them, "Lazy servant, neglecting your master's affairs! And the wealthy man has been waiting for me for so long; we should have gone at night, why are we still here?" The family replied, "Didn't you already diagnose and treat the patient?" Mr. Qiuquan was greatly surprised and exclaimed, "Did I really give him medicine? I must have been truly drunk; I must kill him!" He stamped his foot and quickly untied the boat to return, thinking that if he did not leave, he would be humiliated.

The family hastily untied the mooring ropes, and the host had already sent a servant to wait for Mr. Qiuquan. Upon hearing the departure, the servant immediately entered to report. Soon after, the door opened, and many lanterns were seen on the shore. A message was sent to stop Mr. Wang. Mr. Qiuquan was unsure, thinking, "Is this really possible? It must be deceiving me." However, he had no choice but to follow the servant up the hall, the door closing behind him, his heart still thumping. Once seated, the host repeatedly apologized, saying, "How miraculous is Mr. Qiuquan's medicine!" Mr. Qiuquan then said, "Yesterday, I already understood the general idea, please allow me to examine further." He then entered to examine and checked the remaining medicine, reassuring himself, "Thankfully, I did not make a mistake." He gave him more doses, which cured the illness, and returned with a generous reward. People called him the Drunken Mr. Qiuquan.

The Annalist said: This matter is recorded in the *Wuqing Records*. In a drunken dream, the miraculous effect of the medicine was so great, could there be ghosts or deities within it? Yet it also shows that medical skills are not entirely reliable, and that life and death have their own fate! A smile. The Rắn Snake According to the *Shuijing Zhu* (Commentary on the Waterways Classic): In the Jinchixiu Mountains of Jiaozhi, there is a large snake known as the rắn. It is ten zhang long and has a circumference of seven or eight chi. It often perches on trees waiting for deer and other animals. When they pass by, it lowers its head and wraps around them. After a while, once it has moistened them, it proceeds to eat their heads and horns. After three months, all the bones protrude through the skin. The hill tribesmen, seeing the snake motionless, would use bamboo sticks to skewer it from head to tail, kill it, and eat it, considering it a rare delicacy. Another account suggests that if a woman's clothing is thrown at it, the snake will coil up and not move, making it easier to catch. The *Yiwuzhi* (Record of Strange Things) by Yang of the Southern Regions says: "The rắn is a great snake, both broad and long. Its colors are varied, and its patterns are like brocade. It eats pigs and swallows deer, fattening itself to heal its wounds. At banquets and feasts, it is served as a dish or a drink." This refers to the time when it fattens itself, its fat becoming very rich, making it a delicacy for banquets. Unfortunately, it swallows deer, providing enough for a glutton to feast.

The large python can swallow deer and eat people. It is extremely lustful and picks up women's old trousers, throws them on the ground, and wears them on its head while moving around, finding great pleasure in this act. Those who capture it estimate where it goes in and out and first set up several rows of stakes, narrow enough to just fit its body. A brave man holds an olive stick inside the stakes, and another person outside throws women's skirts and pants to lure the snake. The snake immediately raises its head six or seven feet high and comes to chase. The people retreat into the stakes, and the snake's large body makes it difficult to turn in the narrow space. The people hold sticks and hit the snake, retreating as they hit, and several people take turns. When the snake's head is lowered to the ground, there is no fear. Each strike causes the snake's skin and flesh to shrink. There is a bubble that dies and the blood coagulates (which is the snake's protective gallbladder), greatly reducing its strength. Many are mixed with the real ones, and the real ones are worth double the price. This is what is said in *Lingnan Zaji* and agrees with *Shui Jing Zhu*. However, *Guihai Yuheng Zhi* states that the large python is as big as a pillar and often goes out to chase and eat deer. The guards are good at catching them, with several people full of flowers on their heads, approaching the snake, which likes flowers and will definitely look at them, gradually getting closer and lowering its head. They loudly call "Hong Niang Zi," and the snake lowers its head even more and does not move. A brave man cuts off its head with a big knife, and everyone runs away to watch from a distance. After a while, the snake struggles and jumps, pulling out all the small trees by the road, exhausting its strength and dying, and the whole village feasts on its meat. The method is even more extraordinary. However, what is recorded by Shi Hu is generally based on personal experience and must not be without basis.

According to *Ling Biao Lu Yi*, there are snake-keeping households in Pu'an Prefecture who, every fifth day of the fifth lunar month, carry a large, non-venomous snake into the government office to extract its bile. I have personally witnessed this process, where the snake is placed in a large cage lined with soft grass and coiled on top. Two people carry the snake on the ground and use several sticks to flip its body, preventing it from twisting. They then measure the size of the snake's belly and use a sharp blade to cut it open. The liver and gallbladder protrude, and the bile is cut off, dried in the sun, and prepared for presentation as tribute. The liver is then sewn back into the wound with thread, and the snake is returned to the cage. Some say the snake is carried back and released into rivers and lakes. According to various books, the large, non-venomous snake is so powerful that it cannot be caught alive. However, the account in this record suggests that obtaining it is indeed quite easy. Is this truly the case?

**The Records of Strange Things from the Southern Regions:** The teeth of the python are six to seven inches long. The local people value them highly, believing they ward off misfortune and are beneficial for long journeys. One such tooth can be sold for the price of several oxen. **Picking Longan Fruit** The branches of the longan tree are very soft and brittle. When the fruit is ripe, skilled climbers are hired to ascend and pick it. To prevent them from eating freely, an agreement was made: "Do not stop singing, or we will not pay you." The leaves spread out in a dense canopy, and people sit beneath the green shade, their voices rising and falling in a continuous, murmuring chant. To hear it occasionally is quite pleasing to the ear, but upon reflection, it is amusing in its absurdity. **Grandiose Talk** When I was young, I read the *Biography of Wang Mang*, and I noticed that everything from legal decrees and edicts to the names of officials and regions was copied and imitated from ancient texts, used freely to embellish and decorate his regime—not only did he imitate works such as the *Da Gao*, but he took inspiration from many sources. One can imagine how people of that time accepted it without doubt, in a dazed and痴迷 state. Later, when I read the *Biography of the Later Shu* from *The Book of Song*, I found the following account: Emperor Taizong of Song sent Wang Quanbin and others to attack Shu, and Meng Chang dispatched Wang Zhao Yuan to resist them. Wang Zhao Yuan was fond of reading military treatises and considered himself a master of strategy. When the campaign began from Chengdu, Meng Chang sent Li Hao and others to see him off. Holding an iron如意 in his hand, Wang Zhao Yuan compared himself to Zhuge Liang. Drunk, he said to Li Hao, "This expedition is not merely to defeat the enemy. I shall lead these two or three hundred thousand savage youths and take the Central Plains as easily as turning over one's hand." Soon after, he fought Wang Quanbin once at Sanquan and was defeated, and again at Jianmen Pass, where he was captured. Truly, this is a tale fit for a laugh!

However, throughout history, there have been many such deluded individuals. In the Southern Yan, there was Wang Shi, a man from Laiwu. During the fourth year of the Jianping era of the Southern Yan, Wang Shi deceived thousands of people with his magic, gathering them in the Tai Mountains at Laiwu Valley. He proclaimed himself the Emperor of Peace, appointed officials, titled his father as the Supreme Emperor, his brother Lin as the Commander-in-Chief of the East, and his younger brother Tai as the Commander-in-Chief of the West. The emperor sent the Carriage and Cavalry General Guiyang Wang to suppress and capture him, and he was executed in the capital. At the time of his execution, everyone cursed him for bringing about his own downfall. Someone asked where his father and brothers were, and Wang Shi replied, "The Supreme Emperor is in exile, and the Commander-in-Chiefs of the East and West were killed by rebel forces. Even though I am still alive, what is there to live for?" His wife, Zhao, angrily said to him, "You are exactly where you are because of this mouth of yours. How can you still speak such foolish words on the verge of death?" Wang Shi said, "Why don't you understand the will of heaven? Since ancient times, how could there be an unvanquished nation or an unbroken family?" The executioner hit his mouth with the hilt of the knife, and Wang Shi looked up to the sky and said, "I am about to die, but I will never change my imperial title." This is particularly laughable, and can especially be compared with Wang Mang's words, "Heaven has endowed me with virtue, what can the Han army do to me?"

During the years of the Jia Tai and Kai Xi reigns, Guo Niwei, who held a high position known as "Dianyan," referred to himself as the rebirth of Zhuge Liang. After drinking, he would recite the lines "Three times I visited, with repeated invitations, / Two dynasties I served, opening and saving the state." Chen Jingjun, the military granary official, entertained him by saying, "The mechanical devices of wood oxen and flowing horses, then, will be a burden for you." After the army was already defeated (referring to the Battle of Fuping), he realized he could not recover, and he cried in front of guests. At that time, Peng Fachuang was present, and he told others, "This is a watered-down Zhuge Liang (using 'juice' as a pun for 'duty' or 'position')."

Lu Shi Ke When Lu Shi Ke was a student, he once stayed at the home of a wealthy family in Yinxian County. During the humid season, he ordered a servant to dry the bedding. The servant passed the task to a maid, who took the bedding to the master's concubine's room to dry it. By evening, the maid secretly carried out the concubine's sleeping shoes. Lu Shi Ke, intending to sleep, unfolded the bedding and discovered the shoes, throwing them onto the bed canopy. Later, the master entered Lu Shi Ke's study and saw the shoes. He waited for Lu Shi Ke to leave, then secretly took the shoes with him. By deep night, he secretly ordered the concubine to knock on Lu Shi Ke's door, and followed her with a knife. Lu Shi Ke asked who it was, and the concubine replied in a low voice, "It's me." Lu Shi Ke said, "How can there be a female guest visiting a gentleman at night?" The concubine pleaded again, saying, "Just open the door, I have something to say." Lu Shi Ke responded, "What can a female guest and a gentleman possibly talk about? If there is something to say, come tomorrow with the master. If you don't come, I will immediately hand you over to your master, don't be ashamed to be humiliated." Seeing Lu Shi Ke's resolute and unassailable nature, the master agreed, saying, "Please open the door, I'm here." Once inside, Lu Shi Ke saw the master holding a knife and was greatly alarmed. The master said, "Don't be afraid." He then showed Lu Shi Ke the shoes and explained the situation. Lu Shi Ke smiled and said, "Fortunately, I am not partial, otherwise, your knife would have been stained." The next day, Lu Shi Ke left the house.

After passing the imperial examination in the year of Ji-Chou during the Wanli era, he rose to the position of Dali卿. At that time, many people attached themselves to Wei Shao, but the official remained independent and did not flatter anyone, ending his career with integrity. This event is recorded in the *Wuqing Zhi*. Some say this event is already recorded in *Zi Bu Yu*, where it is attributed to a certain military governor. It is unclear which account is correct. According to *Jing Xin Lu*: Chen Chunzhu was a guest of Jia Sidao, upholding his principles and was disliked by the other guests, including the eunuchs. One day, the concubines competed for favor, and one of them secretly stole a shoe from another concubine and hid it under Chen Chunzhu's bed, intending to accuse both of them. Jia entered the study and saw it, becoming suspicious. At night, he brought this concubine to the study door to lure her. Chen Chunzhu did not respond and then became very angry. Jia then realized he had nothing to worry about and investigated the concubines to find out what happened. Thus, he deeply respected Chen Chunzhu and later appointed him as the military governor of Nan'an. The Yuan dynasty theater plays depicted this event, which is similar to the current account. It seems that at the time, someone may have intentionally created this story, or perhaps it was fabricated. According to the records, it is unclear whether this story is based on real events or is fabricated.

**Xingxing** Non-Non said: "In dense forests and deep waters, fish and birds each have their own happy places. Yet those who are ultimately captured by humans are those who are greedy for the bait." The *Commentary on the Water Classic* describes: "The xingxing is shaped like a yellow dog, and also resembles a badger or hog. It has a human face, with dignified features and a refined, beautiful voice." Chu's Taiyuan Wang Gang said: "The xingxing loves wine and wooden shoes. The villagers placed it in the mountains and valleys. It often moves in groups of several, and upon seeing wine or other items, it knows they were set out by people and takes them. Knowing the name of the person who set them out, it curses, saying, 'You servant wish to kill me, quickly abandon this and go away!' It then returns and says, 'Let me just taste the wine.' After becoming drunk, it takes the shoes and puts them on, and in the end is captured by humans." Lu You's poem reads: "Even when drunk, the xingxing still wears shoes; in autumn, swallows still build nests." This creature's fondness for wine and shoes is also clearly recorded in other texts, so it is not without foundation. According to a Tang dynasty novel: In the Fēngxī region of Wǔpíng Prefecture in Annan, there lived a xingxing. It was as beautiful as a woman, could understand human speech, and remembered past events. Because of its fondness for wine, it was captured through the use of wooden shoes. Hundreds of them were confined together in a cage. When people wanted to eat them, the group themselves selected the fatter ones, and sent them off with tears. At that time, they presented it to the magistrate of Fēngxī, covering it with a handkerchief. The magistrate asked what it was, and the xingxing, speaking from within the cage, said: "Only my servant and a pot of wine!" The magistrate laughed and accepted it. Indeed, the intelligence of this creature is remarkable; it surpasses even the cleverness of Lu Ji’s yellow dog, which delivered messages. Yet in the end, it too was captured because of its love for wine and shoes. The *Book of Rites* says: "The xingxing can speak, but it does not escape from being an animal." Indeed! But is this true only of animals?

**燕妒** In the household of the Niu family in Guangling, a pair of swallows was raising their young. The female was killed by a cat, and the male mourned for a long time before suddenly flying away. Shortly after, he returned with another female to care for the chicks together. The next day, one of the chicks fell to the ground, and by evening, all the chicks had died. Upon inspection, it was found that their throats were filled with hemp seeds, which had actually poisoned them. Alas! Even birds are so jealous of their previous offspring, yet the male was unaware of this, a pitiful sight indeed. Last summer, in the distant Fragrant Study of Shen Liting's home, a pair of swallows had already raised their chicks. One of the females was bitten by a snake. The next day, the male returned with another female to feed the chicks. Soon after, the snake came back, but the male was out and had not returned. The female, startled, flew up and pecked at the snake's eyes. The snake, just as it was about to swallow one of the chicks, could not turn around to bite and quickly spat it out, slithering away. The chick, already on the verge of death, was covered and protected by the female, who occasionally brought drought lotus leaves from the courtyard to feed it. Soon, the chick recovered. From then on, whenever the female went out, she returned quickly, staying by the side of the chicks day and night, and the snake never returned. Thus, this female acted as a heroic protector for the chicks. **戒贪** From the *Jinlouzi*: During the reign of Duke Huan of Qi, he lay in the Pine Rest. A white bird made a continuous, anxious sound, hungry and seeking to be full. The duke opened the green silk kitchen and brought food forward. There were those who knew propriety and did not eat, leaving; those who were content and enjoyed the best parts of the food, leaving; and those who were greedy, sighing and breathing deeply as they ate. When they were full, their stomachs burst. This serves as a warning against greed.

I have seen a mosquito whose abdomen was swollen and had turned red, with its tail continuously dripping blood, yet it still continued to feed. When I drove it away, it settled in the screens and tables, unable to fly again. At that time, did it not also regret its gluttony? **Teacher's Admonition** In our village, there is someone who acts as a wandering spirit. Once, he slept for several days. One day, he suddenly woke up and immediately asked his family, "Did a certain person from our village die yesterday?" His family replied, "Yes. Did you see him in the underworld too?" He said, "Yesterday, at the City God's hall in the Yue Temple, I saw the ghost soldiers take him there. The City God slammed his desk in anger and said, 'What good deeds did you do in the mortal world?' The person knelt and wept, saying, 'I did not dare to do evil in my lifetime.' The City God was angry and said, 'Looking at your rabbit head and rat face, do you have even a drop of ink in your heart? How can you kill people with your cow doctoring, and then pretend to teach books to deceive money and mislead the young?' He ordered the ghost soldiers to pull him down and beat him. The person knelt again and said, 'Although I am called a world-renowned doctor, there has never been a case where I have made a mistake and sought help, so I could not treat life as grass. Only because I was forced by hunger and cold, I temporarily taught children to make a living. The only book I have ever read in my life is the Four Books, and most of the punctuation is incomplete, so the highest fee I ever charged was only two taels. They were mostly the children of kitchen helpers, cart drivers, and those who could not read or write, who were willing to learn from me. They only wanted to save money and did not intend to delve deeply. If it comes to the Four Books, I也不敢胡乱教授，所以还不至于造成大的错误,' indicating a cautious approach to teaching beyond basic knowledge. The City God's anger slightly subsided.

"Judge Gu took the register to inspect it, and when he reached the first line of Cen's entry, it was annotated: 'Mianman (read as bian) huangniao.' The city god angrily said, 'These people should only be sent to the realm of beasts.' He then inspected the next line, which was annotated: 'As for what is truly foul-smelling, it is the smell of evil; as for what is truly beautiful, it is the beauty of goodness.' The city god smiled and said, 'If these two words are read in this way, what would be the meaning?' Cen replied, 'This should be read as four lines, saying that if one is evil, one must be as foul-smelling as an evil stench, then it is truly evil; if one is good, one must be as beautiful as a beautiful person, then it is truly good. Thus, the meanings of good and evil are both sincere.' The city god said, 'Then how should the later text that says 'evil but knows its beauty' be interpreted?' Cen said, 'The word 'evil' in this case should be read as a falling tone. Evil, as a concept, is not considered beautiful by anyone in the world. But according to the *Bencao*, the benefit of human yellow (a medicinal herb) to people is significant. Its taste is more beautiful than that of return. Therefore, Mencius said: 'Evil (sentence-final particle) in that it respects one's uncle.'"

The City God cursed, saying, "You animal! You have been teaching others this way, still claiming not to have misled anyone?" He then ordered that the person be punished by being made into a dog, and let him eat evil to repay it. Cen knelt down and said, "In life, I ate twice as much as others, and had much phlegm and internal heat. Whenever the summer heat caused me to wheeze, my suffering was indescribable. I hope the Great King will forgive me, and punish me by making me a cow instead." The City God was surprised and asked, "What is this again?" Cen replied, "I once read in the *Qian Jia Shi* (A Thousand Poems): 'Cows do not know the scorching sun.' Only cows can withstand the heat." The City God laughed, ordered the ghost soldiers to drag him down, first beat him a hundred times, and then took him back to his hometown, making him be reborn as a cow, to warn those who teach. All the family members did not believe it. The next day, they heard that a neighbor's cow had given birth to a calf, and they went to see it, and indeed, it was true. They playfully called its name, and the calf raised its head, wagged its tail, and barked, as if responding. *Peony* In *Ri Zhi Lu* (Daily Knowledge Record), a person from Shandong carved the *Jin Shi Lu* (Records of Metal and Stone). In Li Qingzhao's *Hou Xu* (Postface), the phrase "In the second year of Shaoxing, the first day of the seventh month of the year of Xu Yi" was used. The person did not know that "Zhuang Yue" comes from *Erya* (Erya), and changed it to "Peony." All the books carved since ancient times are like "Peony."

From the *Fuzhang Lu* (Record of Laughter): In the ninth year of the Shaoxing era (1139 AD), the Henan region returned to Chinese control. Merchants carried steles from the Qin and Han dynasties from Chang'an (modern Xi'an) and sought to sell them to scholars and officials. Wang Xilao obtained a stele, but not a single character could be identified. Wang alone praised it endlessly. A guest asked, "What era does this stele belong to?" Wang could not answer. The guest said, "I know, it is called a 'nameless stele.'" They laughed and parted ways. Today's connoisseurs are largely like the nameless stele. In the *Willow Painting*: In October of the year Xinchou during the Qianlong era (1781 AD), Lu Jingxuan of Xiaoshan served as the magistrate of Yongcheng. In the office, there was an old willow tree that had withered over the years. He ordered workers to cut it down, and the patterns within were like those painted with light ink: rocks on both sides were steep and sharp, on a cliff there was a pine tree with vines entwined around it; an old tree with branches and leaves all hanging downward. Below, there was an old man holding a staff and standing, wearing a tall hat and long sleeves, with his beard and eyebrows clearly visible. His left hand was in his sleeve, resting on his chest, his right foot forward, revealing his shoes, his left foot hidden under his clothes, looking back as if listening to a stream. Even a masterful painter could not surpass this. There are types of wood with natural patterns resembling images, and to find such patterns in a willow tree is something unheard of. The ingenuity of creation, how can it be conceived! In another account from the year Renyin during the Kangxi era, the authorities in Jingkou ordered the construction of warships. In a garden owned by the Liu family in Jiangdu, there was a ginkgo tree, over a hundred years old, which was also felled. When cut open, the grain of the wood showed two images of Guanyin Bodhisattva, exquisitely beautiful and natural. Everyone was astonished and placed it in the Southern Blessing Shrine. It seems as though a divine presence had imbued it, making it even more extraordinary.

A Man from Fujian named Wu Yuchang Bi once visited Hangzhou. He was invited to a banquet at the home of the High Commissioner Jin. The cook prepared a round fish. After it was cooked, it was cut open, revealing a meat sculpture of Guanyin, the bodhisattva of compassion. She wore a golden crown and was beautifully adorned, with eyebrows, eyes, and folds of clothing all looking like a painting. Her right hand hung down, and her left hand pressed down, with her feet stepping on a single lotus flower. All the guests at the table were astonished and regretful, and then ordered the broth to be poured over. This event is recorded in Fan Xie's *Chengdong Zaji*. Is it truly that the bodhisattva appeared to serve as a warning against killing? Other accounts, such as those recorded in *Youyang Zazhu*, tell of Emperor Yang of Sui eating a clam, inside of which was an image of one Buddha and two bodhisattvas. During the reign of Emperor Wen of Tang, inside a turtle was an image of the bodhisattva Guanyin. *Xu Yi Jian Zhi* records that Shi Hao ate a clam, and inside were two images of Buddha, with coiled hair and necklaces, and feet stepping on lotus flowers. *Yi Shi Zi Xie* records that Shao Chongyi cut open a clam, and inside was an image of a bodhisattva. Jian Qu said that a resident of Shuanglin Town cut open a clam, and inside were eight immortals made of pearls. *Yi Jian Bing Zhi* records that Zheng Boying found an image of Guanyin inside a clam in Chuzhou, with a beautiful and dignified appearance, holding a willow branch and a clean vase. Also, inside a crab's belly, he found a ghost judge, with hair standing up, strange and ugly, terrifying. *Jian Gu Ji* records that a resident of Suichang County cut open a turtle, and inside was a monk sitting upright, holding a pearl, wearing neat clothes and shoes. Inside a chicken egg in Tang Xun's household, there was a bodhisattva sitting on a lotus flower. All these accounts still say that immortals and bodhisattvas appeared to serve as a warning against killing.

As for what is recorded in other books, there are accounts of a small Buddha statue found inside a silkworm cocoon, resembling an Arhat Guanyin in meditation posture. There are also reports of a macaque found inside a chicken egg. For such cases, what can one say? Indeed, the rise of such strange phenomena is ultimately beyond ordinary human comprehension. Lake Market In the fourth month of the Jiaqing era, during the year 1800, the stone embankment near the western gate of Gaoyou County, which was by the lake, collapsed. The river commissioner appointed officials to oversee the repair work. Among them was a guest official named Xiah Yoxiang from the provincial government office, who was supervising the laborers as they worked back and forth on the embankment. As the day was drawing to a close, he suddenly saw a city that appeared real in the lake. The city was lush with trees, and a broken shore was spanned by a small bridge. On the bridge, watchtowers, palisades, and拒马 (a type of defensive barrier) were all present. A person was walking quickly on the bridge, holding a large umbrella as if against the wind. Beneath the shade of willow trees, two donkeys were grazing. At that moment, the setting sun was sinking behind the mountains, and the evening clouds were rising in all directions. The golden and jade-like appearance of the city gates in the lake was particularly striking. The water ripples along the embankment resembled silk fabric, interacting with the setting sun to create a dazzling and indescribable scene.

The city was filled with wisps of smoke, towering buildings, and high pagodas, with the sound of bells echoing everywhere. A sudden evening breeze arose, and the distant cityscape of trees, bridges, pavilions, and towers gradually faded and disappeared in an instant, though several hours had already passed. I have heard that in Shandong's Dengzhou and Laizhou, there are sea mirages; in Sichuan's Qingcheng and Qiancheng, there are mountain mirages; and here, there is even a lake mirage, all of which are remarkable sights. The *Ice Mountain Record* When the family of Fen Yi was dispossessed, there was someone who wrote the *Ice Mountain Record* to document the event. Wang Rengyu's *Remnants of the Kaiyuan and Tianbao Eras*: Yang Guozhong's power was so great that ambitious individuals rushed to his door. Zhang Tan, a successful jinshi, was a man of great renown. Someone advised him to pay respects to Yang Guozhong in order to gain honor and prominence. Zhang smiled and said, "You all think that Yang Gong's power is as stable as Mount Tai. In my view, it is like an ice mountain. If there is a time of great brightness and clarity, this mountain will mislead people." Indeed, as he predicted, people saw Zhang's foresight. The following year, he passed the imperial examination and was appointed as a magistrate in Huayin County. At the time, the county magistrate was not a suitable person, but Zhang had administrative skills. Whenever he proposed something, the magistrate would suppress and reject it. Zhang said with determination, "A true man has the ambition to reach the heavens and surpass the world, yet is constrained by a lowly position, as if standing in a low, cramped room where one cannot look up freely." He then left his post and retreated to Mount Song. The *Record* is based on this meaning.

Mount Tai It is said that the inscribed stone pillar at Mount Tai is not actually an inscription, and it is likely that something is hidden within it, such as inscriptions from the Fengshan ceremony or documents sealed with gold and lacquer. Once upon a time, a local official disliked the doubts raised by the people and ordered the stone pillar to be removed. As soon as the cover was moved, the wind and thunder suddenly arose. Although this seems strange, there is no doubt that something is inside. However, the scholar Gu Ningren argues that the *Shiji* (Records of the Grand Historian) and *Hanshu* (Book of Han) only mention setting up a stone pillar, not carving inscriptions on it. This shows that the scholars of old were meticulous in their reading. Nevertheless, the *Jingji Zhi* (Catalogue of Books) in the *Sui Shu* (Book of Sui) mentions a volume called *The Inscriptions of the Stone Pillars Left by Emperor Qin Shi Huang During His Eastern Tour to Kuaiji*, which likely refers to the stone pillar that was set up. Therefore, this statement cannot be taken as a definitive conclusion. Customs of Non-Han Ethnic Groups The *Gaoli Tuteng* (Illustrated Gazetteer of Goryeo) states that it is common for men and women to bathe together in the same river. Among the ethnic groups in the southwestern border regions, there is a ritual called "jumping the moon," which involves having children out of wedlock before formal marriage. This is seen as a difficult aspect of their customs to understand in terms of shame and honor. However, there are also instances of shame and honor among these groups: Among the ethnic groups living in the mountainous regions of western Guangxi, women begin bathing in water in April and stop in September. Men and women often bathe together, and touching someone in a private area is not considered taboo; however, touching someone's breasts is strictly forbidden, and if it happens, they may kill each other in anger. This is because they believe that breasts are part of a woman's role and responsibilities, and they hold this belief very strongly. This is a form of virtue and righteousness.

The Customs of Siam: When a Chinese man has an affair with a woman who is married, her husband is pleased and considers it an honor. Sometimes he invites the Chinese man to drink together, saying that his wife is beautiful, hence the Chinese man is attracted to her. This is a peculiar way of thinking. (I also heard that men in Siam adorn their penises with mirrors, bells, jade, and precious metals; the wealthy use gold and silver, while the poor use copper and tin. When they walk, they make a clear, ringing sound. In weddings, monks welcome and send off the groom. When the groom arrives at the bride's home, a monk places a red sticker on the groom's forehead, calling it "good fortune.") The indigenous people of Taiwan do not know the calendar or perform ancestor worship. Outside of parents, they have no terms for uncles, aunts, nephews, or nieces. They value daughters more than sons. Whether they have children or not, they often engage in mutual exchanges. During the hot months, both men and women sit naked together. In matters of lust, older individuals avoid younger ones, and siblings do not feel ashamed to be together. This is another form of distinction. As for the Uyghurs, if a man marries a beautiful wife, he gives her to his brother. Among the Lao people, when a new bride sees her in-laws, she does not bow but instead offers them water while naked, calling it "respecting the hall." Thus, they have clearly learned the ways of courtesy and yielding. The Portuguese have a custom where those who commit adultery are put to death. However, if a Chinese man has an affair with a Portuguese woman, his hair is shaved, and he is forced to marry her, and he is not allowed to return home. There was once a man who killed his wife's husband. She complained to the local magistrate, who became angry and then married her to the man, saying, "Let your wife also remain a widow." The magistrate's method of judging cases seems to be influenced by some external source.

Shuanglin Ling Clan The epitaph for Shen Xiaozhi of Changshu records: During the period of dynastic change, he once carried his mother through the wilderness. When bandits stole their provisions, his mother refused to give them up, and the bandits, enraged, were about to kill her. Shen Xiaozhi wept and begged to take her place, and they spared him. When a fire broke out in a neighboring house, it threatened to spread to his mother's bedroom. His mother was gravely ill and could not be moved. Shen Xiaozhi cried out to heaven in anguish, and the wind reversed, extinguishing the fire. Later, when his mother was over eighty and fell seriously ill, doctors all said there was no cure. He cut flesh from his thigh to offer, but it did not help. In a dream, a spirit clad in red robes told him, "This illness cannot be treated with the five medicines. The physician Ling, in Shuanglin, can cure it." He was quickly summoned. When Ling arrived, he treated her with acupuncture, and she was miraculously cured. This is recorded in the *Wangxi Collection*. It speaks of Shen Xiaozhi's extraordinary virtues, which were sufficient to move bandits and touch the divine. And yet, the Ling family's fame for acupuncture and moxibustion is not accidental! The descendants of the Ling family have excelled in this profession to this day. Shen Xiaozhi's name was Yu. He died in the 49th year of the Kangxi era, at the age of ninety-four, and was the ancestor of Shen Shu, a scholar-official during the Yongzheng era. Appendix: *Bili Records* Ling Hanzhang was from Huzhou. During the Chenghua era, he was renowned for his miraculous skill in acupuncture and moxibustion, famous throughout Wu and Zhe. The *Two Zhe Records of Notable Figures* describes him as generous and valiant in spirit, treating others' illnesses as if they were his own. When called upon, he would rush to treat patients regardless of the time or weather. With his stone needles, he could cure all ailments. Every morning when he opened his door, dozens or even hundreds of people would come seeking treatment. He never charged the poor, so when he died, his family had no remaining wealth.

Yang Yuan, the Master of Yang Garden Master Yang Yuan was burying his parents. After having chosen the site for burial, the villagers obstructed the burial, and he was unable to proceed. Therefore, he temporarily placed the coffins in the estate and ordered a tenant to guard them. Thieves came and set fire to burn down his estate, and the disaster extended to the two coffins. When the criminals were apprehended, they were executed, and their heads were offered at the tomb. Yet, Master Yang Yuan wore coarse hemp clothing for the rest of his life. His son-in-law, You Jiesi, was young and talented, carrying a backpack to follow as a student. He spoke and acted according to rules, and they got along very well. Master Yang Yuan gave his daughter to him in marriage. When his brother, Shishi, passed the imperial examination and became an official, he indulged in wine and women, and Jiesi followed suit in defiance of his father-in-law. Despite repeated admonitions, he did not reform, and later, he bought a courtesan as a concubine. Master Yang Yuan’s daughter, who had always been well-versed in the teachings of the inner chamber, used poetry and books to advise him. Her husband, finding her words unpleasant, conspired with the concubine to poison her. Master Yang Yuan went to mourn her, seeing the signs of poisoning, and brought the matter to the authorities. The husband was stripped of his official robes, and the concubine was expelled, but the crime of killing his wife was never fully addressed. After the death of his son, his only grandson also died in succession, and the family line ended. The Anonymous Historian said: "Mr. Chen Gumíng, at the age of twenty, served Mr. Yao Zhè’ān. He said, 'Those who are profoundly foolish cannot be changed, like You Jiesi who disgraced Yang Yuan. And Zhou’s son-in-law also disgraced Cheng’an, both of whom were matched by Yang Yuan. This was also a painful matter for my former teacher. Yet, there are indeed people in the world who cannot be reformed or taught; one killed his wife, and the other became a thief—what harm did these two masters suffer?' This argument is true. Yet, how cruel was the fate of Master Yang Yuan! I once discussed this with Menglu, who said, 'This is what is meant by fate.' Alas! Is this truly the case?"

According to the master's chronicle, in the seventh year of the Chongzhen era, the year Jia Xu, when the master was twenty-four years old, he lodged at the home of Yan Shifeng. At that time, literary societies in the southeast were just emerging, and the master and Shifeng agreed not to attend indiscriminately, but rather to refine their writings and conduct through mutual discussion with fellow students such as Qiu Heng. However, the master, in his interactions with esteemed gentlemen such as Yan Yingsheng, Qiu Jixin, and Ling Yu'an, often took the imperial examinations as a warning. When invited to tutor children, they all declined in succession, considering it from a perspective of practical gain. At the age of sixty, Yao Gong Yuhu and his younger brother Lian visited the master in Zhang Peichong's study. Coincidentally, Yu Xi entrusted Zhang Peichong with several dozen volumes of *Donggao Yixuan* to be sent. The boatman carried them up, repeatedly calling them very heavy. The master jokingly said, "These may not be heavy; I consider them as light as goose feathers." Yao then asked, "Can scholarship and the imperial examinations coexist without hindrance? Or must the latter be completely abandoned before one can pursue the former?" The master replied in a serious tone, "As the *Book of Poetry* says: 'The bitter wormwood and the stinkweed wither, while the millet and the sorghum flourish.'" This shows how strict he was with himself.

The Master wrote to Wu Puzhong, saying, "Heaven has bestowed upon me filial piety, and I know the joy of not needing medicine. Reading the *Final Days* chapter, I am continuously moved to tears, truly feeling the same deep sorrow as expressed in the *Ode of the Mother's Grass*. As a son, I find myself in a situation where there is little I can do, and I must be cautious and vigilant, as if standing near a deep abyss or walking on thin ice, just as Zengzi wished. The text says, 'The end is difficult.' And it further explains, 'The end refers not to the death of one's parents, but to the end of one's own life.' Thus, as long as a son lives, it is still a day to serve his parents. I hope to encourage my dear brother to continue striving."

Water Moon Temple Outside the Gate of the North Mountain in Wulin, there stands the Water Moon Temple, which was once the home of the Water Moon Elder. The elder's name was Sun Wen, with the courtesy name Wen Shi and the pseudonym Water Moon. He was a scholar from Kuaiji. After the change of the state, he retired to Hangzhou and named his residence Plum Garden. By nature, he was calm and quiet, never taking even the smallest thing, and occasionally composed short and long lyrics. When asked about his age, he often claimed to be ninety. All his hair had fallen out, and people often called him a monk. When Fan Zhongzhen, the loyal and steadfast official from Shenyang, was young, the elder once placed his hand on his head and said, "This child will establish a banner in our land, and I will still see it." When Fan Zhongzhen became governor of Zhejiang, his mother spoke to him about the elder. They sought him out and came to pay respects, screening off his retinue. They talked for several times. At that time, there were many tigers in Xixi. The elder explained the reason. He said, "Tigers on the hill can be fought, but tigers inside the door are to be avoided." When Fan Zhongzhen became governor of Fujian, the elder saw him off and said, "When the fire behind the ear flares up, must have a plan." Later, Fan Zhongzhen died in the difficult situation of Geng. People began to realize his foresight and rushed to see him. The elder disliked it and avoided them, and no one knows his final destination. The locals thought about him and changed his residence into Water Moon Temple. They made an image of the elder resembling a monk and worshipped him, establishing it as a place of worship. The *Chibei Oudan* called him Water Moon Monk.

The Historian says: The *Xichao Xinyu* also recorded this incident but did not mention the phrases "fire behind the ear" and did not detail that the person was a remnant of the late Ming dynasty. Matters are determined by fate, and the old man knew this, yet he ultimately refused to submit to the new dynasty, just as Gong Xu refused to serve the Yongle Emperor, saying he feared to betray the sorrow at Jin川门. Since ancient times, loyal ministers and filial sons, would they change their resolve because of the times? Speaking from the abdomen: The *Liaozhai Zhiyi* states: There was a thief who was about to be executed, and several steps away, he still turned around and shouted, "What a sharp blade!" This only vividly describes how sharp the blade was. I once mentioned this to others, but they all laughed at it. According to the *Ming Shi: Biography of Yang Weidou*, after the fall of the Ming dynasty, Mr. Yang faced execution without yielding, his head already severed, yet his voice came from his neck, thus the story in *Liaozhai Zhiyi* is not false. Also, during the Eastern Han dynasty, Jia Yong, the governor of Yuzhang, fought against the enemy, lost his head, yet still wore his bow and armor, carried his horse back to camp, and asked the generals, "Is it better to have a head? Or is it better without a head?" The generals replied, "It is better to have a head." Jia Yong said through his abdomen, "It is also good without a head." All such cases are like a drunk falling off a carriage and not dying, because their spirit remains intact. I have heard that Tang Huaqing, the military governor of Jian Nan, was ordered to pacify Duan Zizhang and succeeded. Later, when he led an expedition, he rode out alone into battle, fell into a heavy encirclement, lost his head, yet still approached a stream to wash his hands and face. A washerwoman saw him and said, "Without a head, how can you wash?" Upon hearing this, she fainted. This is evidence of the dispersal of the spirit.

Another account: During the early years of the Zhide era, Wang Mu of Taiyuan served as a deputy general under Lu Min. In a battle in Nanyang, he was defeated, and enemy cavalry pursued him from behind, cutting off his neck with a sword. His tendons and bones were severed, leaving only his throat still connected. Initially, he did not realize he was dead; after an hour, he came to his senses. His head was positioned on his navel, and soon he felt food leaking, so he used his hands to hold it and reattach it to his neck. Soon after, it fell again, and he fainted once more. After a long time, he regained consciousness. After reattaching his head, he tied his hair on both sides to support it, allowing him to sit up. The horse he was riding did not leave him; as Wang Mu stepped into the stirrup with one foot, his left hair tie came loose, and his head fell into his chest. That night, he regained consciousness, reattached his head with his hair, and thought that if the horse lay down, he could mount it. The horse suddenly lay down, and Wang Mu managed to get on. The horse then got up and carried Wang Mu southeast. With both hands, Wang Mu held his cheeks and walked forty miles. Several dozen of his scattered soldiers saw him and helped him to a village, where they later transported him to the army. Wang Mu spent over two hundred days recovering in the city. Around his neck, there was flesh like a finger, and his head remained slightly off-center. This is especially strange because his head fell several times but could still be reattached. During the early years of the Jiaqing era, the Miao rebels incited rebellion in Chu and Shu. The imperial army fought against them in Xiangluping, and the rebels were defeated. One rebel leader was killed by the imperial army, and his head had fallen, but he still held a large axe in a defensive posture. A white vapor rose from his neck, reaching about six meters in length, and after a while, he fell. This might be due to some kind of magic, which is beyond ordinary explanation.

Liu Zizhuang Liu Zizhuang, courtesy name Keyou, was from Huanggang, Hubei. From a young age, he was exceptionally intelligent and could read several lines at a glance. His writing was strange and unrestrained. He passed the provincial examination in the year 1630 (Gengwu) during the reign of Emperor Chongzhen. After being recommended, he dreamed that a divine figure told him, "You must have Zhu Zhiyi as the examiner to pass the imperial examination." Upon arriving in the capital, he once stepped out of his lodging to take a walk and saw several young boys carrying books and passing by his door. One boy stood out in particular, and Liu Zizhuang took his hand and conversed with him. He noticed the boy's book had the name Zhu Zhiyi written on it, and he was greatly surprised. He followed the boy to his home. Zhu Zhiyi's father was a woodworker, and he gave Liu Zizhuang several writing implements as a gift, and they parted ways with great respect. Later, Liu Zizhuang did not participate in the imperial examination due to the chaos caused by bandits. During the imperial examination in 1659 (Jichou) during the reign of Emperor Shunzhi, Zhu Zhiyi was already serving as a co-examiner, and Liu Zizhuang's essay was selected as the top scroll, making him the first-place winner. This story is also found in *Xichao Xinyu* (New Tales of the Kangxi Era). Reading this, one understands that success and failure are fated, and the timing of events is also predetermined, which leaves one feeling moved. Xiong Belong Previously, when candidates presented their responses in the imperial examination, they all used the four-six prose style. During the examination in 1659 (Jichou) under Emperor Shunzhi, the emperor personally presided over the examination and commanded that the traditional four-six prose style should not be used. Liu Zizhuang's response was in accordance with the emperor's wishes, and he was personally selected as the top candidate in the first class, ranking alongside the second-place winner, Xiong Belong. Xiong Belong served as the chief examiner in Zhejiang and had three top candidates in one examination: Shi Dacheng in 1665 (Yiwei), Yan Wosi in 1674 (Jiachen), and Cai Qifu in 1680 (Gengxu). This achievement was highly honored by scholars.

#### 库中画 In the imperial palace of the Ming dynasty, there were paintings stored in the treasury that had been kept hidden for many dynasties. During the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, the emperor wished to open and view them. The eunuchs objected, saying that they had never been opened before. However, the emperor's will was firm, and the eunuchs dared not defy him, so the paintings were opened. Upon inspection, there was nothing inside except for a small red box. When it was brought forward, it bore the inscription "opened on the [year]th day of the [month]th month of the [year]th year of the Chongzhen era." The emperor was surprised that the box had been prepared in advance and found it even more mysterious. When he opened it, it contained only three paintings: one depicted countless civilians facing each other in opposite directions; another showed countless officials and scholars fleeing in panic; and the third depicted a single person with disheveled hair and a naked body hanging from a tree, whose appearance was clearly that of the emperor himself. The eunuchs were visibly shaken, and the emperor left in a state of melancholy. #### 乩书 During the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, the palace would sometimes summon spirits or generals to inquire about future events, and their responses were always accurate. In the past, the spirits would appear immediately, but this time they did not come for a long while. After a long wait, the spirit of Emperor Wu descended and wrote: "All the generals have already been reborn in the human world, so there is no one to respond to your summons." The emperor bowed deeply and asked if the generals who had been reborn were doing anything significant and if there were any who had not yet been born. The spirit wrote: "Only Lord Guan Yu, who received your deep respect, has not yet descended." After writing this, the spirit became silent, and further questions went unanswered.

### 玉人 The people of the Crane Country are three inches tall, traveling eight thousand li in a day, as fast as flying. They are often preyed upon by sea cranes. These people are extremely ingenious and craft a jade figure of themselves, gathering hundreds into a group and congregating in the wilderness near water. The cranes think these figures are small people and swallow them, leading to their own deaths. Later, other cranes see the real figures and are afraid to eat them. There are many in this world who, like a tiger with wings, prey on people. Yet, among them, isn't there someone like the jade figure? Only those who prey on others lack the wisdom of the cranes, so they often follow one another in gluttony without realizing it, and cannot help but be deceived by petty people. ### 天主教 The Lord is named Jesus, a person from the Atlantic. During the reign of the Wanli Emperor, there was an Italian named Matteo Ricci, who sailed nine thousand li to reach the Xiangshan Bay in Guangdong, beginning to spread his teachings in China. He said that Jesus was born in Judea, a country in Asia, also known as the Byzantine Empire, the ancient Great Qin. At that time, it was the second year of Yuanshou in the reign of Emperor Ai of Han, a period spanning one thousand five hundred eighty-one years, until Jesus died in the ninth year of the Wanli reign. The country has existed for over six thousand years since its creation, and the Lord was born in the land where humanity was first created. The emperor was pleased with his distant arrival and gave him generous gifts, thus building the Church of the Lord inside the Xuanwumen Gate in the capital. The church is elaborately and strangely decorated, with a statue of Jesus. To the right is the Holy Mother, who looks like a young girl, holding a child, who is Jesus. Thus, the teachings of the Lord spread widely.

Those who wish to join the salvation, their teacher orders them to drink a bowl of clear water, and their hearts become confused. They are then given fifty taels of white gold, so many people come forward to join. When they return, they are ordered to destroy ancestral tablets, stove gods, and door gods, and to worship a cross-shaped wooden frame. If they encounter anything shaped like a cross, they dare not treat it with disrespect. If a follower dies, the teacher always sends two people to recite scriptures beside the corpse, covers the corpse with cloth, and leaves after inspection. Some believe this is because they steal the two pupils of the eyes. Once there was a very poor man who could not borrow money from anyone. Knowing that joining the group can earn fifty gold pieces, he warned his family in advance, waiting until he got the silver and returned, and gave him medicine to make him vomit. Later, when the man joined the group and returned, he indeed wanted to destroy the god tablets and worship the cross. His family tied his hands and feet, gave him medicine to make him vomit clear water, and finally, a blood clot was vomited out. His family put the blood clot in a water basin, and after a night, the blood dispersed, leaving one thing that did not disperse, which formed a human shape with eyebrows and beard. Upon closer inspection, it was the teacher.

大胆** From the biography of Wu Yingji: After the southern capital was lost, he raised an army to support Jin Sheng. Defeated and fleeing into the mountains, he was captured and died bravely and resolutely. At the time, there was also Wu Han Chao, who raised an army and joined forces with Qiu Zude and Ma Sanheng's armies, successively capturing Jurong, Lishui, Gaocun, Lianyang, Jing, and Taiping counties. In the following year's first month, he attacked Nancheng Ningguo, climbed up the southern wall at night, and the troops were defeated. The people in the city investigated the person who initiated the rebellion, and Han Chao, having already left the city, thinking of his mother and fearing that others would be implicated, entered to say, "I am the one who initiated the rebellion." They cut open his gallbladder, which was three inches long. In this case, Jiang Wei's gallbladder was like a chicken egg, and is not worth mentioning. **项王走马埒** From the *Stone Column Record*: Bian Mountain once had Xiang Yu's horse-pond, horse-stone, and horse-racecourse. Zhu Shao Shi's *Kao Yi* states: Xiang Yu sought refuge in Wu, crossed a large stream, and encountered a strange creature. It would roll up people and animals with its tail and eat them morning and evening. Xiang Yu straddled its back, one hand grasped its neck, two feet held its belly, one hand clung to a tree, and pulled out several large trees.
