At dawn, he saw it was a horse, its body covered with black dragon patterns, and named the stream after it, which is now known as Longxi. Ming dynasty poet Zheng Mingxuan wrote: "Xiang Wang used to race horses, with a golden racecourse on all sides. In his decline, his favorite horse Zui did not move, and he sang mournfully to the red-faced woman."

**Buried Sorrows Collection, Volume Two** **The Water Demon Wu Zhiqi** According to *The Ancient Peaks and Ravines Classic*: Yu the Great, in his efforts to control the floods, visited Tongbei three times. He was met with violent winds and thunder. Angered, Yu summoned all the spirits and commanded the Azure Dragon to pursue and capture the water demon. They captured the water demon of the Huai River's whirlpool, named Wu Zhiqi. His appearance resembled that of an ape or monkey, with a recessed forehead and a high nose. His body was green, his head white, his eyes golden, and his teeth snow-white. He had an elongated neck, stretching a hundred feet, and his strength surpassed that of nine elephants. He could strike and move swiftly, so fast that his movements could not be held in view for long. Yu tried to control him with a child, but failed. He then tried to control him with a magician, but failed. Finally, he entrusted him to Gengchen, who, wielding a halberd, captured him. Wu Zhiqi was restrained with a large chain, his nose pierced with a golden bell, and he was moved to the foot of Gui Mountain in Huaiyang. *The Classic of Mountains and Seas* states: "The water demon enjoys causing thunderstorms. Yu the Great locked him at the foot of Jun Mountain." This is the same creature. During the Tang Dynasty, a censor wanted to see this demon. He had criminals touch the demon's image, and one of them managed to grasp it. Using sixty-four oxen, they pulled the chain to release the demon. As the chain was nearly released, the demon leaped into the air, letting out a loud cry like thunder. The chain, along with the people and oxen, disappeared.

My local censor, Tang Gong Shiji, once served in Huaiyang and told Zhou Menghou of Lize about it. According to the *Commentary on the Waterways Classic*, when Yu the Great was controlling the floods, he reached the Huai River, and the river god appeared, taking the form of a macaque. It dug the ground with its claws, creating water. Yu ordered Gengchen to capture it and lock it beneath Guishan. The *Collection of Hard Gourds* records that when the Ming Taizu passed through Guishan, he ordered a strong man to lift and look at it. The man pulled an iron chain that filled two boats, and a thousand men struggled to lift it, revealing only an old monkey with long hair covering its body. It roared loudly once and suddenly plunged into the water. The *Miscellaneous Records of Youyang* recounts that when Emperor Xuanzong performed a ritual at Mount Tai, Zhang Shuo was the envoy for the ritual. Zhang Shuo's son-in-law, Zheng Yi, was originally a ninth-rank official. By old custom, after the ritual, all officials from the Three Excellencies would be promoted one rank. Only Zheng Yi, due to Zhang Shuo, was suddenly promoted to the fifth rank and was also granted a red robe. There was a grand feast. The next day, Emperor Xuanzong saw Zheng Yi's rank soar and asked him about it. Zheng Yi had no answer. Huang Fanchuo said, "This is the power of Mount Tai." Nowadays, people refer to a father-in-law as Mount Tai, which may have originated from this. Face-Like Sore Once upon a time, a merchant from the south of the Yangtze had a sore like a face grow on his left arm. Initially, it caused no pain, and he drank and ate like a person. Sometimes, someone would jokingly drip wine into its mouth, and its face would turn red. It ate everything. If it ate too much, a lump would rise on its arm, as if there was a stomach inside it. If it didn't eat, one arm would become paralyzed. A doctor taught him to try various medicines, all kinds of minerals, stones, herbs, and trees were given to it. Until it came to the bulb of the fritillary plant, the sore then furrowed its brow and closed its eyes. The merchant was delighted and said, "This must cure it!" He then used a small reed tube to destroy its mouth and pour the medicine into it. After several days, it formed a scab and healed.

Chen Goushan Chen Goushan, courtesy name Zhao Lun, passed the imperial examination in the year 1730 during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng. In the early years of the Qianlong era, he was recommended and appointed to the Hanlin Academy, eventually serving as the Chief Magistrate of the Capital Province. Throughout his life, he was approachable and easy to get along with, always praising others for their minor virtues. Those who came to him for advice on poetry and prose received the utmost praise, which is why people enjoyed being close to him. His calligraphy was inspired by the *Lantingji Xu* (Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion), capturing the essence in a simple yet profound way. Liang Shanzhou, the Attendant Lecturer, commented: "In this dynasty, people are not famous for calligraphy, but those whose calligraphy will definitely be passed down are only Chen Wenjian, courtesy name Yuanlong, and Chen Goushan, Mr. Goushan." Burying Silkworms In the town, the Wu family raised silkworms every year. That year, due to an excess of silkworms and a shortage of mulberry leaves, they could not feed them adequately, so they buried more than ten baskets of silkworms in an underground pit. They sent three servants to sail to the market to buy mulberry leaves. On their way back, a large carp suddenly jumped into the boat, delighting the three men, who took it home. Passing through Zhaolin, the local official noticed the small boat moving quickly with two oars and pursued them. Upon searching, they found nothing else, but in the forehold, there was a human leg. Questioning the three men, they were all completely unaware of its origin. The local official immediately bound them and took them to the provincial governor, subjecting them to severe torture. He interrogated them about the location of the body and head, and under intense pressure, the three men vaguely admitted that they had seen the body buried in the family's vacant land. The officials then ordered the guards to take them to their home, where they dug it up. It turned out to be the place where the silkworms were buried. However, the silkworms were nowhere to be found, only one body, which was intact except for one missing leg. The evidence matched, and so the three men and the family head were punished for their crime. The story is recorded in the *Wuqing Zhi*.

The historian said: Heaven and Earth bestow life as their virtue, yet those who bury silkworms have a hardened heart. However, during times when silkworms are abundant and mulberry leaves are expensive, there are those who bury their silkworms and sell the leaves instead. One has not heard of them immediately receiving severe retribution. But the Wu family alone suffered this extraordinary calamity, because of their cruelty. Therefore, their accumulated evil deeds must have been even more severe than burying silkworms. This is the origin of their karmic retribution, which must have its cause. I have heard old men say: In the past, there was a village farmer who, because the leaves were valuable, discarded all his silkworms. His son's wife secretly hid several baskets of silkworms. The farmer had several acres of mulberry fields, and the leaves were still there. The farmer's son, having no leaves to gather, secretly went out at night to collect them. The farmer happened to be patrolling his fields in the darkness and, mistaking them for another thief, thrust his spear at them, killing him instantly. When he realized it was his own son, he was filled with grief and anger and hanged himself. His wife and daughter-in-law wailed until dawn and also hanged themselves, and the entire family was wiped out. The farmer's act of killing his son was indeed an act of mistaken identity, but consider that even if it had been someone else, they would have been no more than thieves stealing mulberry leaves. What crime could possibly warrant such a death? How cruel and heartless was he? Heaven used his hand to kill his son! The severity of the retribution, how could it not be feared?

Note: The burial of silkworms is already recorded in Huang Fu Mei’s *San Shui Xiao Du*, but in that account, the person involved was Wang Gongzhi, a resident of North Village, Cijiandian, Xin’an County. When he sold the silkworms, he received three thousand coins and bought a pig’s shoulder and some cakes to take home. Upon reaching Huian Gate, the gate official noticed dark blood smeared on the ground near his bag and questioned him. Gongzhi explained what he had purchased and requested that the bag be searched. When it was opened, it contained only a human arm, as if freshly severed. He was then sent to the *jushou* (city commander). The *jushou* forwarded the case to the governor of Henan for investigation. Gongzhi confessed truthfully, and the governor ordered officials to take him back to the village, where they gathered neighbors and witnesses to obtain statements confirming that Wang had indeed buried the silkworms and had no other wrongdoing. When the silkworm pit was dug up, a dead body wrapped in silk cloth was found, missing its left arm. When the arm was attached to the body, it fit perfectly. This was reported to the governor, who declared that although Gongzhi had committed no act of murder, silkworms are divine creatures of heaven and earth, the source of silk and cloth; therefore, to exterminate them is no different from killing a person. He thus ordered that Gongzhi be publicly beaten to death in the marketplace. This account is largely the same as the one above. What is recorded in *The Annals* is probably based on hearsay.

The Dog That Paid Off a Debt In a certain town, there was a man who kept a dog. Every night, the dog would cross the water to guard and stay overnight at the home of a certain family in Henan. One day, the man called the dog and scolded it, saying, "You eat my food and yet guard for others. Tomorrow I will surely find someone to kill you and sell you." That night, the man dreamed that the dog stood upright and barked, saying, "I once owed money to a family in Henan, so I have been guarding every night to repay the debt. Now I only owe thirteen coins. After repaying, I will no longer cross the river and swear to repay the great kindness of my master." The next morning, the man called the dog to the front and tied thirteen coins around its neck, saying, "I dreamed of you saying that, so now I am returning the money, and you can avoid crossing the river again." The dog lowered its head to accept the command, then took the money and threw it at the family's house before returning. From then on, the dog no longer went back. Later, the man went to visit his daughter, got drunk, and returned deeper into the night. He lost his footing and drowned in a pond. The dog barked loudly and jumped in, carrying its clothes and dragging it to the shore. The dog then jumped to the house and hit the door with its head, waking the master's wife. She followed the dog to the pond side and saw the man lying stiff and not awake. She helped him to the house, and he woke up late in the evening. The man told the reason, and his wife said, "Before, I dreamed the dog said it would repay the kindness, and now it has indeed not broken its word."

After several months, a fire unexpectedly broke out in the house while the entire family was asleep. The dog again went to someone's bedroom, hitting the door with its head while barking. The couple woke up in alarm and saw that the flames were about to reach the house. They managed to extinguish the fire with emergency measures. Later, the dog died, and the owner buried it in a coffin. This story is recorded in Mr. Yang Zhou's *Fruit of Actions and Observations*. Alas, how could a dog remember and repay its benefactor in such a way? It seems to be an animal in appearance but not in heart. I therefore excerpt it to serve as a warning to those in the world who betray their benefactors and masters. In *The Strange Tales of the East*, there is a story about Xu Yuanhui, a scholar from LePing. His father dreamed of a guest in black clothes coming to say, "I borrowed three hundred coins from you yesterday, and now I return them." Before he could ask who the person was or when the loan was made, he woke up. He usually kept more than ten ducks. That day, one returned, and among them was a black one. The little boy thought it belonged to someone else and tried to drive it away. The duck circled around, left an egg, and then left. From then on, for a month, every day it was the same. In total, it laid thirty eggs and then never came back. They never knew who the owner was. Calculating its value, it was exactly three hundred coins. It seems that the one who borrowed money did not forget to repay, even among animals, this often happens. How can humans be worse than animals?

Peeling Skin At the end of the Chongzhen era, a Daoist practitioner recounted: During the Xizong era, he once traveled to the capital. Five people were drinking together in an inn. One of them loudly spoke of the evil of Wei Zhongxian, saying that he would soon fail. The other four either remained silent or were startled, advising him to speak cautiously. This person said, "Although Wei Zhongxian is powerful, he will surely not peel my skin, so what have I to fear?" Until midnight, when he was sound asleep, suddenly someone knocked on the door, shone a light on his face, and immediately took him away. Soon after, the other four were also taken in, and they saw the captured person with his hands and feet nailed to the door. Wei Zhongxian told the four, "This person said he couldn't be peeled, so let's try it now." He immediately ordered asphalt to be poured over his entire body and then struck him with a hammer. Soon, his entire body was peeled, and his skin remained intact as if it were a person. The other four were terrified to the point of death. Wei Zhongxian gave each of them five gold coins to calm their nerves and then let them go. This is recorded in the *Xingcun Lu* (Record of Survival). Alas! The cruelty of Wei Zhongxian was truly unprecedented in history.

Yet there are things that power cannot reach. *Ear Xin* records: On the third month of the year Dingmao, the birthday of Wei Zhongxian, all the high officials and ministers gathered. Suddenly, a monk appeared wearing a headband and a coarse robe, carrying a staff made of vine and a whisk, and knocked at the door to request an audience. The gatekeeper shouted at him, saying, "How many venerable elders and great ministers have been waiting all day. They cannot be seen; how dare you, a vagrant, want to see my lord on his birthday?" The monk replied, "I am a poor friend of Wei Gong. Today, I wish to speak a word in person to wish for a thousand years of life." The gatekeeper dared not report this, and lifted a gourd club and axes, pointing at the monk's head, cursing and driving him away, saying, "You dare to speak recklessly without restraint. Fortunately, it is his birthday today; if it were another day, you would be severely punished." The monk struck the drum with his staff, and everyone was startled. They followed by pushing him inside, saying, "This monk requested an audience, but was not allowed, and struck the drum擅自, causing an offense to the imperial majesty."

The Daoist bowed deeply and spoke sternly: "It has been a long time since we last met; today I am fortunate to see you again. Now that you have reached the pinnacle of wealth and power, could you have forgotten me?" Wei Zhongxian flew into a rage and said, "You dare to act so recklessly, a heretical Daoist! How could I associate with you?" He ordered his attendants to bind the Daoist and deliver him to the Office of Punishment for strict investigation. The Daoist replied, "I am renowned for my ability to judge character and fortune, having seen many people. Do you not recognize that you have the appearance of a thief and the heart of a beast? My only intention is to save the lives of many loyal ministers and righteous men." Pointing a finger to the heavens, he said, "You can deceive the emperor and the people, but can you deceive the heavens? I will witness your suffering, torn into small pieces, to the point where even dogs and pigs would not eat your remains. Could you possibly kill me?" With a sudden leap, his bindings broke, his sleeves waved in the air, and the entire assembly was startled as he suddenly disappeared. This event, like the heretical Daoist during Zhang Xianzhong's court in *The Continuation of the New Records of the Sui and Tang Dynasties*, is sufficient to show that the tyrant's fierce power has no effect, and it is somewhat pleasing to the mind.

#### Historical Anecdotes **Note:** The practice of skinning people alive is unheard of from ancient times. Only in wild histories is it recorded that Jing Qing intended to carry out Yu Rang's plan. The Yongle Emperor searched and found a sword, ordering Jing Qing to be skinned and his skin tied to the gate of Chang'an. The next morning, when the emperor passed by, the tie suddenly broke, making it appear as if Jing Qing had violated the emperor's carriage. Thus, the emperor ordered the skin to be stored in the treasury. However, the account of Jing Qing's death is not consistent. Only Zhang Xianzhong once used this method; if the skinning was not completed before the person died, the executioner would be skinned instead. It seems that the method was not properly understood. How cruel Wei Zhongxian was! How different is he from the bandit Zhang Xianzhong! #### Miracle Cure **Miracle Cure:** The *Qi Xiu Leigao* (Collection of Seven Revisions) states: During the end of the Yuan Dynasty, Xu Tongjian, a magistrate from Houtu Village in Tongxiang, had always admired Lu Dongbin (the Eight Immortals of the Wine Cup). He worshipped him daily. One day, he developed a boil on his back, and he was near death. Despite his condition, he still managed to rise and worship Lu Dongbin. He noticed a piece of white paper under a water jug, which contained a poem: "Flying dust from the tomb turns to golden flakes, White jade sprouts from the petals. The master takes one pound, the minister takes four, Mix and take, and it will heal the wound." He believed it was a divine prescription but did not know what "golden flakes" and "white jade" referred to. He then summoned Lu Dongbin and asked about rhubarb and angelica, to which Lu Dongbin replied, "Yes." After taking the medicine, Xu Tongjian was cured. Later, he used this method to treat others, and all were effective.

Xu Wuzi passed down his family tradition to his son-in-law, the Shen family, who have continued to use it to make a living to this day. People come from hundreds of miles away to buy medicine, without a day of rest. The Shen family is large and divided into dozens of branches, but only those who live under the Great Banyan Tree of the direct lineage see the medicine work. Shen's son once studied under my friend Xu Yuanpan and heard that his medicine now includes pangolin scales, angelica root hairs, and honeysuckle. However, since there is so much rhubarb, it is used indiscriminately for both cold and hot diseases, which may also be harmful. Yet, people continue to come in a steady stream, and the medicine works uniquely for those under the Great Banyan Tree. Could this not be a sign of divine favor? So it is said. However, the Shen family has seen its decline during the Jiaqing era, as I have witnessed. When they were at their peak, there was a man named Geng Wen who was especially skilled in surgery, earning him the reputation of being like Hua Tuo. Even now, as medical knowledge revives, people still come from a hundred miles away to seek his help, though it is not known that he specializes in treating people with this particular formula. As for those who are especially famous in the medical profession today, their name is Tai, who is a disciple of my father-in-law, Mr. Zhang Menglu. According to the biography, at that time, the Censor who was known for his boldness, Tong and Chen E, were feared throughout the court for their demeanor.

E, whose courtesy name was Kezhong, was from Panyu. During the Yongle period, he was selected through the local examination to enter the Imperial Academy and was appointed as a censor in the Department of Criminal Affairs. Whenever he presented matters to the emperor, his voice was loud like a bell. The emperor ordered him to be starved for several days, but he continued to speak loudly, saying, "This is what I was born with." He was often called "the loud scholar." Once, he spoke out against the emperor's will and was ordered to be buried alive in the Hall of Heavenly Purity, with only his head left exposed. After seven days, he did not die and was pardoned and returned to his position. E was known for his wit and humor. When he was being buried, he sighed and said to those present, "I didn't expect to die in such a large pot today!" Someone asked him why he had survived, and he replied, "Don't you know that? The court buries people in pots, to make them die quickly." Those who buried him followed his words, and he was able to survive by stretching and bending. It is said that those who bury people cover them with earth up to their chests, causing them to suffocate and die. If only their heads are left exposed, they will be unable to bear it for long, and how could they survive for seven days without dying? **Lü Zhongyi's Biography** Lín Lù said: When his mother was pregnant with him, she dreamed of a divine being with a feathered canopy and music, descending from the clouds and directly into her arms, and then he was born. As a young man, he was distinguished for his noble spirit and extensive learning, excelling in Jiangxi. After completing his studies, people in the four directions referred to his writing style as "Xiling Style." When he was listed among the talented, the Duke of Loyalty and Integrity appeared in his dreams and spoke to him. The conversation was mostly secret, and no one could understand it. Shen Jun Ding, who had recently died and then revived, said he saw E and others assisting the underworld in making decisions, just as Wang Xiancheng did. Alas! Loyalty and filial piety are the highest virtues of a person. Only by not being ashamed before others can one become a god, which is not surprising.

According to: The gentleman's name is Pei, his courtesy name is Kunting, and his pseudonym is Bulou, from Qiantang. He had six brothers, with the eldest being Qi and the youngest being Jie, all of whom were already renowned before him. From a young age, he valued righteousness and honor, and if his intentions were slightly offended, he would shed tears and vow to die. His mother Qu and grandmother deeply loved and pitied him. As he grew older, his brothers' fame increased, and together with his influence in the southeast, they promoted the teachings of the east and west. Chen Geshi Dazun once said, "I associate with the Lu family, much like Kong Rong among the Jiqun." At sixteen, he was admitted as a student. In the year Ji Mao, he passed the local examination and paid respects to his grandmother in her hall. His mother happily said, "Your father and uncle sang 'Deer Cry' just as if it were yesterday. I am nearly eighty years old, yet I still see you achieve fame. The state's thoughts are deep, so strive hard!" The following year, he passed the imperial examination and became a Jinshi. The gentleman was tall and dignified, often secluding himself to read books. He engaged closely with many scholars in the creation of ancient literary works. His network spanned the entire country, and he enjoyed guiding and supporting younger scholars. However, he was known for his straightforwardness in pointing out others' mistakes, and those with evil intentions would hold their breath and retreat when they saw him. Once, while traveling to Moling with Mr. Xiangwu Lu, they visited the temple of Fang Zhengxue and the temple of Xu Changgong, a loyal minister. A guest presented Mr. Xiangwu with a bow and arrows. As Mr. Xiangwu was composing a poem, the gentleman said solemnly, "Watching the land sink into chaos, I have nothing to contribute. With your talent, you should ride into battle to defeat the enemy and then compose a dispatch, which would be quite satisfying." They stayed at an inn, reading history daily and drinking heavily for a month. One night, his body gradually shortened to about three or four inches, but after a long time, he was able to return to his normal height.

In the year Jia Shen, the rebel leader Li Zicheng invaded the imperial capital, facing north and crying out loudly, hoping to reach the emperor. His wife quickly stopped him, saying, "You have always been well-read. Have you not heard of the events during the Jin and Song dynasties? There is still hope." Soon after, he went to Jiankang, was appointed to the Ministry of Rites, and served as the deputy to Xiong Ruilin, who was a censor. He carried the imperial seal to perform a sacrifice at the Huai River. Xiong was known for his straightforward and honest advice, and when they discussed current affairs, the man became even more frustrated. The following year, in the year Yi You, rebel troops retreated along the river. The man and his brothers supported their mother in Yanguan, and he sent his son Fan Chao to follow them. The provincial capital was in chaos, so he took refuge in the pine forest of Huangshan. He visited his old friend Chen Jun, and they held hands and wept, saying, "We are about to part ways." Chen tried to stop him, but the man said, "Even if I die, it would not benefit the country, so I might as well fulfill my duty." Upon returning home, his wife ordered the servants to watch over him. He smiled and said, "Death cannot be brought back to life. My mother is old, and I should avoid the world and live in seclusion like Tao Yuanming." Shortly after, he closed the door and hanged himself, but he was rescued by guests. On another day, he got up early in the morning, called for his writing tools and clothes, knelt five times facing north and three times facing south, and gave a sock rope to two servants, saying, "You know my intentions, you can help me." He then sat on a large bed and calmly hanged himself to death. On the table, he left three letters: one for his mother, one for his brothers, and one for his old friends. He was twenty-eight years old at the time.

The woman vowed to die with her husband and fell from a building, as if held by a divine force; she also endured starvation for ten days but did not die. Her mother-in-law said, "It is as if Heaven wants you to live; to defy Heaven would be inauspicious." Thus, she did not die. The husband's brother, Qian, and his younger brother, Jie, were also steadfast in their adherence to moral principles and were among the loyal remnant of the old regime. Less than a year after the husband's death, Tanchen, a government official, committed suicide by drowning himself. Chen Gong, the censor-in-chief, drowned himself along with his wife and concubines. Chen Gong had previously offended the common people, as he had criticized them in his official correspondence. Xiong Gong entered Fujian and was suspected by Zheng Zhilong, leading to both of them drowning at sea. A traveler from Hainan reported that Yao Gong Qi Yun had committed suicide by decapitation. When Fang Gong wrote to the censorate, Qi Yun had advised against it, but Fang did not listen, and both ultimately died. Alas! These four individuals can be called true friends of Fang Gong. At the same time, Wang Dao Kun, a fellow official from the same province, also committed suicide upon hearing of Fang Gong's death. In Jiangdong, Fang Gong was posthumously given the title "Zhongyi" (Loyal and Brave). Dong Gongshu, a Ministry of Revenue official, said, "Two people died together, was it because Wang Dao Kun was not a candidate for the imperial examinations?" Thus, Wang Dao Kun was posthumously given the title "Jiemin" (Devoted and Compassionate).

A Strange Creature In the region of Chu, a scholar-official named a Xianlian came from the mountains into the city. Due to a tiger menace, he was accompanied by two hunters carrying iron forks. At dusk, he took a rest at a post station, when suddenly a tiger roared and came. The two hunters led the scholar to a tree in front of the pavilion and held their forks, fighting the tiger and killing it. Another tiger, accompanied by two cubs, arrived, and the two hunters exhausted all their strength and died. The scholar was just terrified when a moment later, a creature like a small dog appeared, with white fur and red hair, golden eyes, and ran like the wind. It ran straight ahead and bit the three tigers. The three tigers lay down and dared not move, and all died. Each creature ate a little of their brains, and the first to die smelled but did not eat. In a moment, it went to the tree, looked at the scholar and barked loudly, jumped up with a leap, and suddenly fell into the vines at the bottom of the cliff, caught in the air and could not escape. The scholar was terrified, thought to himself that waiting to die was foolish, better to kill it first, so he went down from the tree, took the fork and stabbed it to death. He carried it to County Magistrate [Name], who took its skin and made it into a collar, so that snow would not touch the clothes. Indeed, tyrannical governance is more fearsome than tigers, and the cruelty of harsh officials is all due to greed. If this creature has already eaten three tigers, and still is greedy and insatiable, leading to trapping itself in a net, it can also serve as a warning. According to, this is similar to what is recorded in *The Book of Curious Things* about a foreigner bringing a fierce beast like a dog. However, that creature could eat tigers, while this one wanted to eat humans. Also, *The Book of Lost Chapters* states: a flying dog that can eat tigers and leopards. But this creature seems unable to fly, what kind of creature is it?

Wang Shizhen, in his *Lung-shu Yu-wen* (Miscellaneous Records of the Western Sichuan), states: The *jiaotuan* (角端) is said to be produced in Wawu Mountain, does not harm people, and only eats tigers and leopards. Mountain monks always keep them to protect themselves. According to *Zhonghua Gujin Zhi* (Notes on Chinese History and Antiquities): The people of the Chu Sou country offered a *shu* dog, which can fly and eat tigers and leopards. This is how the *shu* dog is described as the *jiaotuan*. I refer to *Yishu·Wanghui Jie* (Lost Books of Han: Explanation of the Meeting of the Kings): The Chu Sou people offered a *shu* dog. The *shu* dog, also known as the *lu* dog, is likely another name for the *shu* dog. Initially, there was no mention of the *jiaotuan*. *Er Ya* (Classic of Rites) states: The *jiu* resembles a horse with one horn. The *qilin* has a deer body, cow tail, horse feet; yellow color, round hooves, one horn, with flesh at the tip of the horn. The *jiaotuan* is indeed a type of *qilin*, but why are they discussed together? Furthermore, during the reign of Emperor Wu of Han, a person from the north of Da Yuan offered an object, as big as a dog, whose sound could startle people. Chickens and dogs run away when they hear it, and it was called a fierce beast. The emperor was surprised by its small size. When it was released into the park, the emperor wanted tigers and leopards to eat it. When tigers saw this beast, they immediately lowered their heads to the ground. The emperor thought the tigers were lowering their heads to attack, but this beast was very happy to see the tigers, licking its lips and wagging its tail, and went straight to stand on the tigers' heads, and urinated on the tigers' faces. The tigers then closed their eyes and lay down, not daring to move. After the beast finished, it left, and the tigers dragged their tails away. When the beast looked back, the tigers closed their eyes. I once heard my ancestors say that tigers fear small dogs. Small dogs are smaller than large dogs. When tigers see them, they lie down and do not move. Then the dogs urinate around them, and the tigers cannot move an inch. It is likely this beast.

The Palace Examination Response The palace examination response from Wujin County, titled "Wen Jie Gong," written in the twenty-third year of the Wanli era (1595 AD), was retrieved by the author when he served in the Ministry of Rites and is still preserved at home to this day. Each line of the original text contains thirty-two characters. In provincial and metropolitan examinations, both horizontal and vertical red threads are used as guiding lines, but in the palace examination, only vertical lines are used. The rationale behind this system is that the length of the responses can vary, allowing for varying character density without issue. However, in contemporary practice, palace examination responses are typically written with lines of twenty-two characters, which deviates from the original system and thus loses the fundamental principle. Furthermore, before the fiftieth year of the Qianlong era (1785 AD), co-examiners still evaluated candidates based on their knowledge of the classics. The oral test for poetry composition was held in the second round but is now moved to the first round, and the officials responsible for grading no longer have titles based on the Five Classics. This change is due to the fact that all scholars study all five classics. However, I have seen earlier scholars who specialize in one classic and have handwritten copies of it, thoroughly studying its origins and interpretations to the point where mastering one classic makes them knowledgeable about all five. Nowadays, scholars focus only on the prescribed texts and current literary trends, neglecting the classics. Often, candidates do not know the source of the questions during the examination, showing a lack of deep knowledge. How lamentable is this!

Appendix In the thirty-ninth year of the Kangxi era, the Censor-in-Attendance Man Jin submitted a memorandum detailing the accumulated corruptions in the imperial examination system, and the Governor-General Guo Xiu submitted a memorandum detailing the problems in the educational system. Both memorandums were sent to the Nine Ministers for discussion. After the discussion, an order was issued to record and show the memorandums to the provincial governors Li Guangdi and Hu Peng, and the Governor-Generals Zhang Penghe and Guo Xiu. Li Guangdi proposed three measures to improve the imperial examination system and four measures to improve the educational system. In conclusion, he said: Recently, educational officials have generally been negligent in their duties, leading to students neglecting classical studies and ancient texts. Even the Four Books, which are fundamental texts, cannot be memorized and recited. Students only read a hundred or so contemporary essays, imitating and copying others' work, relying on luck for success throughout their lives, which is far from the way the state should cultivate and achieve success. Last year, an imperial decree was issued to educational officials, instructing children to enter school and to also use the text "Xiaoxue Lun" (A Treatise on Primary Education). At that time, young students gained new knowledge and experiences, suddenly understanding ancient meanings. This is clear evidence of the effectiveness of primary education in attracting students. However, if they cannot recite books fluently, they have not fully grasped the material. It is advisable to instruct educational officials to admit students who can fluently recite classical texts and primary education materials, explain the Four Books, and have a basic understanding of the structure and logic. If they can also recite three or five classics, they should be given a stipend as an incentive. Furthermore, since students in primary education are already required to be familiar with primary education to set a good example for young minds, the topics for the imperial examination essays for secondary students and those taking the imperial examinations should not be limited to the "Classic of Filial Piety," which is repetitive and unoriginal. It seems appropriate to also include the "New Book of the Heart" and the "Comprehensive Mirror to Aid in Government" to encourage broad-minded and knowledgeable individuals.

The memorial was submitted and passed down to the nine ministers, where it was deliberated alongside three other memorials by Zhang Penghe. For the provincial examination, separate official examination numbers were compiled, with a quota of nine civilian entries and one official entry. This decision was made by the emperor's will, and Guo Guangdi particularly supported it. The examination topics were set on *Taitu Shuo* (The Explanation of the Supreme Ultimate), *Tongshu* (Comprehensive Book), *Ximing* (Western Inscription), and *Zhengyi* (Official Interpretations) simultaneously. Alas! Since the Ming dynasty, the scholarly customs have declined, and society has gradually deteriorated. This memorandum is appended to show that the state's laws are not inherently flawed, but rather, there are good laws but no good people to enforce them, leading to long-standing problems that cannot be reversed, and the laws are soon abandoned and not discussed. How to find such virtuous men to save the situation and bring it closer to the ancient way! Furthermore, *Jian An Man Bi* (Miscellaneous Notes of the Restraint Pavilion) says: When I was young, I studied for the imperial examinations and had no printed versions of examination essays. There were book dealers who would visit the homes of my friends who were preparing for the examinations, copying *Dengchuang Xiake* (Essays Written Under the Lamp) in dozens of pieces. Each essay was copied onto about 20-30 sheets of paper. When they came to my family school, I selected a few essays, each with two or three variations. I remember that Tang Xianzu won the top prize in the provincial examination, and his essays were also printed by his student Cai Ying from Wuxi and his relative by marriage. Xue Fangshan won the top prize in the provincial examination, and his three examination papers were printed with movable type at Donghu Academy by my student Qian Mengwang from Changshu, whom I encouraged. I have not heard of any commercially printed versions. Today, everywhere one looks, there are commercially printed books, which is also a reflection of the superficial and substantive aspects of society.

Yang Zichang said: The compilation of the "Eighteen Examiners' Works" began with the *Gouxuan Lu* in 1592 (the third year of the Wanli reign), which included marginal annotations. It was from Fang Wangzhong (Shisū) that the selection of model essays began. After 1619 (the year of Jǐmǎo), there were four types of publications: model essays, which included the works of candidates and examiners; examiners' works, which included the writings of the ten examiners; candidate submissions, which included the works of those who passed the preliminary examination; and society works, which included the compositions produced by students in literary societies. For one examination cycle, hundreds of copies of the examiners' works were published, all originating from Suzhou and Hangzhou, and were sold by merchants from Central China and the North. People across the country only knew that this could bring them fame and wealth, calling it learning and talent, while neglecting all other books. In the past, Qiu Jun, during the prosperous times of the Tianshun and Chenghua reigns, had already observed that there were scholars who passed the examinations without knowing the titles of historical texts, the sequence of dynasties, or the radicals of Chinese characters. Across the country, people focused solely on the "Eighteen Examiners' Works," reading them for three to five years, and upon passing the examination, ignorant children would stand beside the high-ranking officials, while the ways of literature and governance were discarded like worn-out hats. Alas! The prevalence of the eight-legged essay led to the decline of the Six Classics, and the rise of the "Eighteen Examiners' Works" led to the neglect of the Twenty-One Histories. This is what *Rizhilu* laments.

I have examined what Wen Zhuang said, and at that time there were already draft versions of works. Now, there are even more texts from the early days of the dynasty that are no longer considered, regardless of the periods of Jiajing and Longqing. This is another change in the world's customs. **Tui Bei Tu** According to *The History of the Song Dynasty* (*Ting Shi*), during the Tang Dynasty, Li Chunfeng composed the *Tui Bei Tu*. During the Five Dynasties period of chaos, princes and lords rose to power, and people harbored hopes, so the study of such prophecies became more intense. The prophecy of "opening one's mouth to draw a bow" led the states of Wu and Yue to name their children after it, without realizing that it was a sign of the glorious beginning of the Zhou dynasty. When the Song Dynasty was established, the signs of divine mandate were particularly famous. Emperor Taizu ascended the throne and issued an edict to ban prophetic books, fearing that they would confuse the people's minds and lead to increased legal penalties. However, the *Tui Bei Tu* had been circulating for several centuries, and there were many copies in private hands, making it impossible to collect them all. The authorities were concerned about this. One day, Zhao Kang Wang, the Prince of Kaifeng, presented a report on a case involving numerous violations, stating that it was impossible to punish everyone. The emperor ordered the retrieval of old copies of the *Tui Bei Tu*. All the verified ones were jumbled and mixed together. A hundred copies were made to circulate alongside the existing ones. Thus, the transmitters were confused about the order, and no one knew which was correct. Occasionally, some copies still exist, but they are no longer verified and are also discarded without being kept. This is why the current transmission is so varied and mixed.

During the early years of the Xuanhe period in the Song Dynasty, the imperial workshop produced brocade, which was called "Pandì Táo" (Scattered Peaches). Another type of brocade and lacquer hat was made in the shape of two peaches, and was named "Bìng Táo" (Together Peaches). This style was imitated throughout the land. Fragrances were also called "Pèi Xiāng" (Worn Fragrance). When the Jurchen people invaded the capital, regardless of rank, all people fled from their homes and were taken away by the Jin, fulfilling this prophecy. Is it recorded in the *Tui Bei Tu* (Back of the Neck Chart)? Regarding Li Zicheng's death, according to the local gazetteer of Li Prefecture by He Lin, unofficial histories record that he died at Luo Gong Mountain in Tongcheng, while the official history of the Ming Dynasty records that he died at Jiu Gong Mountain in Tongcheng, both believing he was killed by villagers. However, Luo Gong Mountain is actually located in Qianyang, and Jiu Gong Mountain is actually located in Tongshan County. The statements about Tongcheng are all incorrect.

Professor Sun told me that Li Zicheng actually fled to Lishui Prefecture, reaching Qinghua Post Station, and then with more than ten horsemen, they fled to Guniu Dam (in present-day Anfu County), and then rode off again, alone fleeing to Jia Mountain in Shimen County and becoming a monk. His grave is still there, they say. I was surprised by this, so I specially went to Jia Mountain and saw a stone pagoda beside the temple, covered with a roof. The pagoda face had the large inscription "Fengtian Yuheshang" (the Jade Monk Fengtian). In front of it was a stele, written by his disciple, Wěifú, stating that the monk did not know his surname. An old monk, over seventy years old, could still recount old stories of Jia Mountain. He said that the monk entered the temple and served in the Buddhist order at the beginning of the Shunzhi era, not saying where he came from, but his voice sounded like that of a Westerner. A few years later, another monk came, saying he was his disciple, a member of the Chan school, called Wěifú, from Jiangnan. He served the monk very respectfully. The monk died in February of the year Jia Yin of the Kangxi era, about seventy years old. On his deathbed, he left some words for Wěifú. At that time, he was young and did not hear them. The temple still has his portrait. I ordered it to be fetched and looked at, and it was a portrait of a man with high cheekbones, deep eyes, hawk-like eyes, and a scorpion-like nose, with a terrifying appearance, which matches exactly what is recorded in the *History of Ming*. Li Zicheng usurped the title of Fengtian Changyi Dàyuánshuài (the Great Marshal of the Righteous Army under the Mandate of Heaven) and later called himself the New Shun King. His self-proclaimed title as the Jade Monk Fengtian, it seems, was a way to disguise himself. And Wěifú, as a disciple of the Chan school, served him very respectfully. Could he have been one of his former ministers or attendants? The *History of Ming* also records that when Li Zicheng was killed on Jiugong Mountain, our soldiers sent someone to identify his body, but it had rotted and could not be recognized. But the old monk personally heard his cough, and his Western accent was quite unusual.

The epitaph of Li Zicheng, written by Jiang Binggu (courtesy name Yu Zhi), is as follows. According to the *Lizhou Gazetteer*, it refutes the error in the *Ming History* regarding "Tongcheng," and thus the mistake about "Luogong Mountain" does not need further argument. The references cited are also precise. However, based on earlier historical accounts, it is clear that Li Zicheng was killed by villagers. The account states that the villagers killed Li Zicheng, stripped his body, and found a dragon robe, a golden seal, and a missing eye. The villagers were greatly shocked and suspected that they had killed Li Zicheng. This account is not without basis. This old monk knew the beginning of the monk's entry into the temple and the exact date of his death, and must have remembered his appearance. It is regrettable that Professor Sun did not inquire further into the details at the time. According to the biography of He Tengjiao: Li Jin (a relative of Li Zicheng, later given the name Chi Xin) and Gao Bizheng (the brother of Li Zicheng's wife) returned to He Tengjiao in Jingzhou. He Tengjiao submitted a memorial to the throne, stating that "the main culprit has been eliminated, slightly relieving the anger of the gods and people, and it is appropriate to thank the gods and ancestors." The Tang Emperor was greatly pleased and immediately appointed He Tengjiao as a Grand Secretary of the Eastern Palace and Minister of War, and bestowed upon him the title of Duke Dingxing. However, there was doubt about whether Li Zicheng was truly dead. He Tengjiao stated, "Li Zicheng is dead, but his body is already decomposed." He did not claim credit and firmly declined the title, but the emperor refused. At the time, there were also doubts about whether Li Zicheng was still alive, hence this account is included in the biography. The account of the Qing court sending officials to verify the body, and the report by the Prince of Duying that "defected soldiers said Li Zicheng had fled to Jiugong Mountain, was trapped by villagers, hanged himself, and his body was too decomposed to identify," are consistent. However, if Li Zicheng was not dead, then who was the person who wore the dragon robe, carried the golden seal, and had a missing eye?

Mistress Xu Zhu Yuan According to Shi Yanke's *Continuation of the Shishi Family Traditions and Customs*, Mistress Xu, whose name was Zhu Yuan and courtesy name was Shan Huai, was from Guangling. At the age of thirteen, she became the wife of the late father. Prior to this, a northern official wished to marry her, and she wept, saying, "They are descendants of wealth and nobility, and likely to indulge in a life of luxury and idleness. Where would I go? I wish to serve a cultured man, like Chao Yun of Dongpo, that would be enough." Upon hearing this, the late father felt compassion for her and agreed to the marriage. Four years later, she gave birth to a younger sister who died shortly after, and she did not conceive again. In the year Ji Wei, the late father changed his position to become a teacher in the imperial court, and Mistress Xu wrote a poem, which included the lines, "If heaven could understand my suffering, the harsh conditions of the north would all turn southward." Her stepmother, Lady Li, ordered her eldest son, Chun, to take her to the capital.

Another three years passed, and my father fell ill. My elder brother returned after coughing up blood, and I also returned to the south. The stepmother burned incense and prayed to heaven, cutting her own flesh to offer, and vowed to the gods, saying, "The master's lifelong virtue has been accumulated in his body. Even if he cannot recover, he should wait for his son to say goodbye. Otherwise, as a great scholar, to die at the hands of a concubine would make the master's eyes not close peacefully." She then cried all night, like this for three days and nights. On the third evening, there was a white light like a ribbon falling from the roof, and a strange fragrance arose before the bed. My father suddenly revived, and from then on, he could eat porridge. This happened on the 27th day of the third month of the year Guihai. I heard the news and rushed to see him. Another seventy days later, my father died. The stepmother mourned and offered sacrifices every day as if he were still alive, for five years without change, and finally died of depression. She was then buried together with the master in Luosichong. The stepmother could write poetry, and often burned her drafts. After her death, several poems were found and included in *Xueyuji*. *From the collection of miscellaneous notes, Xiao Fenchang Zashi*: Zhu Yuan once wrote a poem titled *Sending to the North*, which says, "The wind is strong, pulling at our parting; the lamp is dim, and I cannot sleep. With no wings, how can I reach you?" Yu Shan responded with, "Do not resent the long separation; endure the cold and sleep alone. My old soul is about to break, and I cannot dream of you." He also responded to a poem sent to Xiao Jing, saying, "We promised to grow old together by the green mountains, but fate drives us and does not allow us to rest. Sending the letter with the embroidered words to the mirror, I tearfully look at my sorrowful face alone."

According to the Master's poetry and prose, all are gentle, kind, and sincere, his character mirroring his person, nothing but the product of his true nature and spirit. Hence, his scholarly elegance and romantic sensibility mark him as a timeless lover of love, fitting indeed for such a figure. And as for the depth of Zhu Yuan's affection, it is truly worthy of the Master’s Chao Yun. *A Defense of the Biography of Mao Wenlong* Mao Wenlong's seizure of Pi Island to restrain the Ming court was not without aid to the strategy of the time. Yet, from the moment he established his command on the island and resisted the Ming, he suffered defeat in every battle. Furthermore, his crimes of wasting military funds, violating regulations, and killing surrendering soldiers were already frequently spoken of by the court officials of the time. Even the twelve crimes that Yuan Chonghuan listed in person were stated with certainty. Thus, his arrogance and coercive behavior indeed justified the possibility of his execution. Hence, in the year Tianqi, when the ministers debated whether to retain or dismiss the commanders, Zhang Heming alone said: “If Wang Huazhen departs, Mao Wenlong will certainly not be of use, and the soldiers of Liaodong will surely collapse.” From this, it is clear that Mao was proud and difficult to control. And the *Biography of Yuan Chonghuan* also states: although the situation at Dongjiang was sufficient to restrain the enemy, Mao Wenlong himself lacked great strategy and often suffered defeat, while the annual cost of his campaigns was immeasurable. Moreover, he was solely concerned with expanding trade and dealing in banned goods; in times of peace, his business was selling ginseng and trading cloth, and in times of crisis, he was rarely of use. Even if his crimes did not amount to treason, at the meeting on the two islands, Yuan Chonghuan first proposed to reform the military system and establish supervisory officials, but Mao Wenlong angrily refused. Yuan Chonghuan then resolved to execute him. Thus, his death was entirely self-inflicted.

Especially the fact that Yuan Chonghuan acted alone in executing someone, it was enough to provide grounds for criticism. The biography describes the execution of Yu Wenlong, which differs slightly from the official history, and the writing is somewhat weak, with language that is coarse and nearly colloquial. To its narration of "sitting close and whispering" for several lines, and the later "fulfilling a promise of a hundred cuts," it reads like a child's performance of a village play. This is because the *Chonghuan Biography* contains the statement, "If I cannot succeed, the emperor will also execute me as he did Yu Wenlong," and at the end of the biography, "Yuan Chonghuan falsely executed Yu Wenlong, and now the emperor realizes that he should have executed Yuan Chonghuan." These statements are linked to this, and it is said to be a case of retribution. However, it is not known that Yuan Chonghuan's execution was originally determined by Qian Longxi in the case of the traitors. Thus, Wei Zhongxian's remaining faction used the charges of "unilaterally initiating peace talks and acting alone in executing a high-ranking general" to implicate both Yuan Chonghuan and Qian Longxi. When Emperor Sizong executed Yuan Chonghuan, he also implicated the current dynasty. Nevertheless, this shows that Yuan Chonghuan's genuine efforts to serve the state were seen as a threat by the current government and the traitors. Therefore, his execution is recorded in history as a matter of widespread injustice. The claim that each piece of flesh fell and people competed to buy and eat it is excessive; how could Yuan Chonghuan's life have come to this? Could the author truly be a descendant of Yu Wenlong? However, this biography was intended to vent anger on Yu Wenlong, but it ended up becoming a baseless and fictionalized account.

Xu Quanyi once advocated for the innocence of Xiong Tingbi, and he is likely a man of integrity. His memorial is fully recorded in the biography of Xiong Tingbi, but it is not recorded in the *Ming Shi* (official history of the Ming dynasty). Perhaps the author used this as a basis for their work. However, one should take the official history as the basis. According to the *Lie Huang Xiao Shi* (a historical text), Maowenlong feared the emperor's wisdom and sought a way to establish himself. He then communicated with the Qing, offering to donate three million taels of silver to exchange for the territories of Jincheng and Fuxing, and reported the restoration of these territories. An agreement was made. When Yuan Chonghuan was appointed as the commander-in-chief and left the border, the emperor summoned him to discuss strategies, and Yuan Chonghuan answered that he could pacify Liaodong in five years. Upon taking office, he discovered that Maowenlong had an agreement. Yuan Chonghuan quickly sent a lama to the Qing, offering generous benefits, to persuade Maowenlong to abandon his agreement and join him. However, the Qing most valued oaths and insisted on not doing so. The lama said, "Now, the only option is to kill Maowenlong. For the Qing, it would not be a breach of the agreement, and for me, it would be a success." Yuan Chonghuan then used the pretext of reviewing troops to go directly to Pidao. Maowenlong held a grand banquet. The next day, when Maowenlong paid respects, Yuan Chonghuan also hosted a banquet. Halfway through the banquet, Yuan Chonghuan claimed to have a secret imperial order and captured and killed Maowenlong on the spot. At that time, Maowenlong's troops were strictly disciplined in camp, and the soldiers also dared not violate. After the matter was settled, Yuan Chonghuan then reported to the court, which temporarily tolerated it. Later, the Qing demanded a bribe, and Yuan Chonghuan specifically requested an additional three million taels, claiming that after five years, all of Liaodong would be restored, which was a plan for one-time effort and lasting peace. Court deliberations all insisted on not agreeing, and allowed the Qing to invade, leading to the Zunhua incident. This is why Yuan Chonghuan killed Maowenlong, originally for the sake of competing for merit. However, his biography does not record this, perhaps they did not see this record.

### Appendix: The Biography of Mao Wenlong Mao Zhihuang wrote *The Biography of General Mao*, recounting that Mao Wenlong was born on the fourteenth day of a certain month in the fourth year of Wanli [1576] in Songsheng Village, Qiantang. He was handsome, with well-groomed facial hair and bright, piercing eyes. His personality was unrestrained, and he did not concern himself with farming or business; instead, he enjoyed discussing military matters. Once, while drinking with a group of people in a tavern, he struck the table in excitement and declared, "I will not rest until I am made a marquis." Everyone laughed at him. At the age of thirty, he traveled to Yan, but did not achieve success, so he then went to Liaodong. The military commander of Liaodong took him into his service, appointing him as an officer in the navy and gradually promoting him to a high-ranking position. Later, through the recommendation of Wang Huazhen, he was granted hundreds of blank orders, allowing him to act independently. This was during the first year of Tianqi [1621]. At this point, he led his one hundred and ninety-seven followers eastward to occupy Piao Island. Piao Island, formerly part of Korea, is surrounded by mountains on all sides, with only a small area on the western side accessible by water. Upon securing the island, the regions of Jin, Fu, Hai, and Gai were all shaken. The court then appointed him as the Commander-in-Chief, bestowed upon him the imperial sword, promoted him to Left General, and further honored him with the title of Taibao. He was also granted the title for three generations, and his son was given the position of Commander in the Imperial Guard.

于是，公益自奋励，筑城修楼橹，立火炮为守御具，又建府铁山，立文庙，设学，诸生得附北直隶山东乡试，有中式者。屯田铸钱，通商舶，为长久计。遇敌敢战，屡捷，出奇无穷。尝战于大石岭，矢来如雨，再易马，皆射死。夜半，公登山入废庙，顾见庑下有黑马，遂跨之出。马行甚疾，敌望之，皆辟易。天明还军，军士皆欢呼。及下马，则一黑虎跳跃而去。众大惊曰：“将军天人也！” 丁卯冬，有时贵人膺召入都，与所亲客言别。问曰：“方今以何事为亟？”会此客与公私隙，故为沉吟曰：“东岛大可虞。”初，公所招集士已十余万，日费朝廷数千金，饷不时发，公屡上疏，仍不发。最后公疏云：“脱使士伍一朝脱巾而呼，臣虽万死，不能禁其离心，如国事何？”廷议已疑其要胁，而时贵适入，时袁崇焕新起经略，驻辽左，时贵阴令图之。

The Chief Administrator of Garrison Agriculture, Xu Quanyi, reflecting on the great achievements of the public official, was angered by the many errors in court discussions. He then submitted a memorial outlining four points that could not be understood, stating: "The Guan Ning region, a single garrison, spends three million in silver and one million three hundred thousand in rice each year. Now, Piji Island, from the Tianqi Emperor's second year to the seventh year, has spent a total of one million nine hundred thousand in silver, plus some extra, and over nine hundred thousand stones of rice and beans. Yet, there is still much criticism about excessive expenditure? This is the first point that cannot be understood. The Guan Ning region can only be seen for four hundred li, yet it has as many as eighteen thousand soldiers. Piji Island, with its jurisdiction over over twenty islands, spans a vast expanse of one thousand li. Without a large number of troops, how can it maintain communication and coordinate support? Now, Wu Sangui uses only fifteen thousand soldiers, yet it is said that in reality, only twenty-eight thousand are actually there, the rest being falsely claimed expenses. This is the second point that cannot be understood. Wu Sangui's wife and children have long returned to Zhejiang, perhaps also with the intention of seeking land and property like Wang Jian, yet there is still concern that he may lose control. This is the third point that cannot be understood. If Piji Island is considered a strategically important location, and yet we rely on and trust Wu Sangui while obstructing his supply of provisions, it is as if we are abandoning him to the enemy. Even if we say that Wu Sangui's life is not worth sparing, once Piji Island is lost, the interior will certainly be in danger. Instead of worrying about this, we are worried day and night about Wu Sangui. This is the fourth point that cannot be understood. The rest of the arguments in defense of Wu Sangui amount to over one thousand two hundred words. He also said, 'I dare to guarantee that Wu Sangui has no ulterior motives using my three sons and one grandson as collateral.' Yet, no one understands."

Yuan Chonghuan then summoned Hong Chengchou to meet him on Shuangdao Island. Shuangdao Island is west of Pidao Island. Yuan Chonghuan said, "I have a secret matter to discuss." Hong Chengchou sailed forth calmly and also wished to use this opportunity to detail the military rations. At the time, many in the army were suspicious and advised him to bring more followers. Hong Chengchou replied, "I am in charge of the eastern front. If he does not follow the imperial order, how dare he kill me? If there is indeed an imperial order, even with many people, what would it help? It would only increase suspicion and discord. Have you not heard of Guo Ziyi attending a banquet hosted by Yu Chao'en?" After they met at an ancient temple, Yuan Chonghuan told Hong Chengchou, "What I want to say to you, others should not hear." The two dismissed their attendants and entered the temple alone, with only a scholar accompanying Yuan Chonghuan. Yuan Chonghuan glanced back and said, "This is a remarkable strategist in my entourage, who has always accompanied me." They then walked into the temple together. The scholar was short and stocky, with a short knife tucked in his sleeve. After they sat down, Yuan Chonghuan moved closer to speak to Hong Chengchou. After a long while, he suddenly said, "Today I wish to sever your head, General." Hong Chengchou laughed and said, "Do not jest." Yuan Chonghuan replied, "I have received a secret imperial order." He took out a piece of paper from his sleeve, which turned out to be a forged imperial order. Hong Chengchou was suspicious, and Yuan Chonghuan said, "If I were to kill you with one stroke, I will repay you a hundred strokes in the future." Hong Chengchou immediately knelt down, tears and sobs without a word. The scholar then drew his knife and killed Hong Chengchou. This occurred on June 5, 1629, when Hong Chengchou was aged 54.

Cao Chunhuan had already killed the general, and the general had an adopted son named Chenglu, whom he raised as his own son. Chenglu served alongside his adoptive father on Piji Island, holding the rank of deputy general. Upon hearing of the incident, he abandoned his official post and returned to Hangzhou. Cao Chunhuan captured him, subjected him to torture, and forced him to falsely confess to conspiring with his father, Yu Wenlong, to rebel. This was likely done to exonerate himself from the crime of unauthorized execution. Chenglu took paper and ink and wrote the four characters "Yue family father and son" in large letters. Everyone was filled with sorrow and anger, and Cao Chunhuan also changed color. Eventually, he was executed. The officers and soldiers on Piji Island then collectively prepared a coffin for the general and carried it northeast. When the coffin reached the sea, it would not move, and the boat flowed backward westward. The officers and soldiers could do nothing about it, so they collectively bowed to it and floated it out to sea. They all went northeast, and Piji Island became desolate. The loss of the left arm began here. Xu Quanyi again submitted a memorial to clear the general's name, but it was not reported. Quanyi then resigned and returned home. Soon after, military documents were sent in abundance, and the capital was greatly alarmed. The court realized that the general had indeed been wrongfully executed and also greatly valued his achievements. Cao Chunhuan was captured and executed by slicing on the market. Every time a piece of flesh fell, people competed to buy and eat it. The hundred-blade curse was fulfilled here. At the same time, high-ranking officials also incurred guilt. The general was strict in his role as a commander, always trustworthy in rewards and punishments, and shared the hardships and joys with his soldiers. He had the style of an ancient famous general. However, he relied on his own abilities and never gave gifts to powerful officials. If someone sent him a thousand taels of silver, he would require a hundred catties of ginseng in return. The general would only return the equivalent value, and therefore, he also caused his own misfortune. Tieshan and Piji Island both built temples to honor the general. The remaining people of Liaodong, some with their wives and children, came to the temples, having no place to go, and cried out, hanging themselves at the temple gates.

埋忧续集 卷一 刘綎 刘少保綎，字省吾。以都督家居。时有贼窃发宁州，势张甚。巡抚遣县令郡守请救，少保辞以疾，复命藩臬往请，坚辞。皆大怒。命医往验，诈则将参之。医至，则奄奄床褥也。众惶急，策无出。未几，忽报刘将军破贼归矣。众大骇，谓：“将军出，吾属固不能知，亦何施此狡狯为？”曰：“贼为陈友谅之裔，蓄谋数传以俟衅。今发不易遏，若知某往，必大备，故密扑之。此兵法也。”众乃服。 时方右文，每公会，坐少保诸生下。郡绅士有公宴，醵金不给，辄目少保字呼曰：“省吾以办此。”少保恒什佰于众输办。或酒酣，令家卒驰马娱宾。少保兴发，往往上马舞双刀。观者但见白气旋绕眩目，不辨其面。虽奇其艺，亦但作戏玩观也。

少保子念述，矫捷有父风。然少保袖箭为绝艺，透坚甲，及五六十步；念述止及二十步许，不能穿札，勇不如也。 少保有女亦勇，嫁于某，奁具丰盛。有盗数十，突围其家，尽室惶恐。女命婢取软甲披之，率婢挥刀出杀贼。贼不能支，遁去。 按《明史》列少保平缅、平罗雄、平播酋、平倭、平倮功盖详，而遗平宁州事，以寇一发即灭耳。然其出奇之功大矣。至若时俗鄙武，里有达官，缘与少保结婚，至削籍。明之不振有由矣。 按：少保最善拔距，能纵跃十丈，横跃十丈。拔距者，《左传》谓“魏犫距跃三百，曲踊三百”，《汉书》谓“甘延寿少以良家子为羽林，善骑射、投石、拔距，尝超逾羽林亭楼”是也。

又按：此篇见《张瓜田集》。原本篇末言“《明史》列少保平缅、平罗雄、平播酋、平倭、平朝鲜，平倮功”，似有误，盖少保平倭时，本与朝鲜兵合也。今特为删此三字。 黄石斋 祟祯时，余中丞集与谭友夏结社金陵。适石斋黄公来游，与订交，意颇洽。黄公造次必于礼，诸公心向之，而苦其拘也，思试之。妓顾氏，国色也，聪慧通书史。抚节按歌，见者莫不心醉。一日大雨雪，觞黄公于余氏园。召顾佐酒，公意色无忤。诸公更劝酬，剧饮大醉。送公卧，特设榻上枕衾、茵席各一，使顾尽弛亵衣。随键户，诸公伺焉。公惊起，索衣不得，因引衾自覆荐，而命顾以茵卧。茵厚且狭，不可转，乃使就衾。顾遂昵就公，公徐曰：“无庸。”侧身内向，息数十转即安寝。漏下四鼓，觉，转面向外。顾佯寝无觉，而以体傍公，公酣寝如初。诘旦，顾出，具言其状。且曰：“公等为名士，赋诗饮酒，行是乐而已矣。为圣为佛，成忠成孝，终归黄公。”

及明亡，执于金陵。在狱中，日诵《尚书》、《周易》，数日貌加丰。正命之前夕，有老狱卒持针线向公而泣曰：“是我事主之终事也！”公曰：“吾正而毙，是为考终。汝何哀？”故人持酒肉与诀，饮啖如平时。酣寝达旦，盥漱更衣，谓仆某曰：“曩某以卷索书。吾既许之，言不可旷也。”和墨伸纸，先小楷，次行书。幅甚长，乃以大字竟之。加印章，乃出就刑。其卷藏金陵某家。 顾氏自接公，自怼归某官。李自成破京师，顾氏谓其夫能死，我先就缢。夫不能用。

外史氏曰：此《望溪文集》所纪黄公轶事，与左忠毅公并书者也。夫古来忠臣义士，莫不以天下为己任。即至时丁板荡，世际沧桑，犹将以一身力扶阳九，不得已而以一死报国，其意固以为未堪塞责也。故当其从容授命，即忠义之名，有不忍言，而何有于身家，更何有于声色货利？余读佛书，迦叶曰：“金刚之身，非世间火所能烧。”又《瑜伽论》曰：“魔有四女，端正无伦。共来菩萨前，呈诸姿态。菩萨以义心定力，四女皆变老丑，羞惭而退。”盖理之不胜夫欲，足令贲、育失其勇，良、平失其智，惟仙、佛为能制之。然仙佛一切不动，而圣贤则有动有静。以左公罹祸之惨，凛凛数言，至今犹有生气。使其平居有如顾氏者，而与之键户同卧起，谓能动其一顾哉！此先生发潜阐微意也。至黄公临命数语，则分定固然，亦二公之所同也。然此岂二公始念哉？此则可为二公痛哭者矣。
对缢 《如是我闻》：京师有富室吕氏娶妇者，男女并韶秀，亲串皆望若神仙。窥其意态，夫妇亦甚相悦。次日天晓，门不启。穴窗窥之，则左右相对缢，视其衾，已合欢矣。婢媪皆曰：“是昨日已卸装矣，何又着盛服而死耶？”此狱虽皋陶不能听矣。

按花庵《中兴绝妙词选》：钱塘吴礼三，字子和，有《顺受老人词》五卷。有陶氏者，与王生情好甚笃。计生时虽暂为萍水之聚，而死后终必长离，因于月夜共沉西湖。赋《霜天晓角》吊之云：“连环易缺，难解同心结。痴呆佳人才子，情缘重，怕离别。意切，人路绝，共沉烟水阔。荡漾香魂何处？长桥月，短桥月。”事亦载《西湖志》。然则天下固有此一种情痴。吕氏夫妇既在合欢之后，得毋亦为情死耶？ 生祭 明崇祯十五年，洪承畴为我朝所败，时传其已殉难，崇祯帝赐祭十六坛，御制祭文以旌之。其后，我朝兵下江南，洪又经略江南川湖等省。从入关，有土人迎而请见，洪纳之。其人入而长跪，出袖中御制祭文朗诵一过，大哭而去。

按：承畴之才，在明末诸臣中，似犹可任以兵事。史中所纪战功，亦有可观者。然黄梨洲先生尝议其所叙战功之多诬，则有不可尽信者矣。《檐曝杂记》言：承畴兵败时，其子弟在家，已刻行状散吊客。崇祯帝方祭十四坛，而承畴生降之信至。后金声起兵徽州，与门人江天一俱败。承畴谕令生降，天一诵御制祭文以愧之。其后从本朝归没于京师，其子弟又刻行状，不复叙前朝事，即从本朝入关序起。有轻薄子得其两行状，订为一本，以作笑端云。 《明史》又言：崇祯十四年，大清兵围松山。承畴与邱民仰誓死固守，外援不至，刍粮并竭。至明年二月，已围半年矣。力不支，城破，承畴降。民仰不屈，死，赠右副都御史，赐祭六坛，官为营葬，命建祠都城，与承畴并列。帝将亲临致祭，后闻承畴降，乃止。 熊襄愍轶事

《全谢山集》载：始宁倪生为予言：其尊人曾从里中仓桥陈氏见其先世《秋曹日录》一书。其人在熹庙时尝为狱官。凡魏阉所杀君子，不下东厂而下刑部者，皆载其狱中事。 其言襄憨自入狱，一饮一食，阉皆令狱官以帖子报知。然襄愍亦无所异。其卧用一藤枕，不分寒署，未尝去身。每晚人静，再拜礼北辰，则取此藤枕供之，莫能知其意也。或以问襄愍，亦笑不答。已而刑有日，襄愍神色不变，手出遗疏，犹为上言边事，又作绝命词。其疏稿为西曹郎所遏，曰：“囚安得上书？”襄愍曰：“此赵高语也。（原注：缺十二字）圣朝安得有此？”怡然就刃。 时奉有传首九边之旨，西曹郎俄录其首，则法场中空无所见，但一藤枕。大骇，相戒勿泄。密报魏阉，则命取熊氏子弟家人拷问，大索，竟无所得。魏阉计无所出，遂秘其事。其九边所传之首，非真颅也。魏阉败后，公子兆璧连疏请公首归葬蒲州，亦明知其非公首，特借以消此冤案耳。

此说在明野史中，俱未之及。吾谓李公映碧《三垣笔记》极言襄愍临刑之惨，与此不符。然陈氏乃亲见者，当不诬也。（蒲州大学士韩爌也） 按：史稿但纪襄愍保辽之功，而不言其通术数。惟于万历三十五年巡按辽东时，岁大旱，行部金州，祷城隍神，约三日不雨，毁其庙。及至广宁，逾三日，大书白牌，封剑，使使往斩之。未至，风雷大作，雨如注。辽人以为神。据此，则狱中之事自非无稽也。 地震 《天变述略》：五月初六日，哈哒门火神庙庙祝见火神飒飒行动，势将下殿，忙拈香跪告曰：“火神老爷，外边天早，切不可走动。”火神举足欲出，庙祝哀哭抱住。方推阻间，而震声旋举矣。 有一绍兴周吏目之弟，因兄荣选。思做公弟，到京方三日，从菜市口买一蓝纱褶，摇摆而还。途遇六人，拜揖尚未完，头忽飞去，陷入墙内寸许。眼睛飞在对门墙上。粘住犹动。眉毛又粘在一处。其六人者无恙。

粤东会馆路口，有蒙师开学，童子三十二人。一响之后，先生学生俱不见。又，宣府新推总兵在元宏寺街，一响，连人及长班七人俱不见。所伤男女，俱赤体寸丝不挂，不知何故。有长班于方震时，弁帽衣袴鞋袜，一霎俱无。一人因压伤一腿卧地，见妇人赤体而过：有以瓦遮阴户者，有以半条脚带掩者，有被半条褥子者，有被一幅被单者，顷刻得数十人。是人又痛又笑。 庆宏寺街有女轿过，一响掀去轿顶。女全身衣尽去，赤体在轿，竟尔无恙。惟冯相公夫人单裤奔走街心，然亦仅见矣。 长安街一带，从空飞堕人头，或眉毛和鼻，或连一额，纷纷而下。大木飞至密云石驸马街，五千斤大石狮子飞出顺城门外。震后有人来告，衣服俱飘至西山，挂于树梢。昌平县教场中衣服成堆，人家器皿金钱首饰俱有。而德胜门外堕落人头人臂尤多。

先是，五月初一日，山东济南知府往城隍庙行香。及庙门，忽然知府皂隶俱各昏迷。有一皂隶之妻来看其夫，见其前夫死已多年矣，乃在庙当差。前夫曰：“庙里进去不得，天下城隍在此造册。” 《传异记》：宋熙宁中，恩州武城县有旋风自西南来，发屋拔木。县令一门及人民俱卷入云霄中，坠地死者不计其数。近道光庚寅之岁，直隶一带震裂不下千里，压死者以万计，然皆未有吹去衣服及肢体者。而此记言之凿凿如此。考《明史·帝纪》及《五行志》，并无五月初六日之变。然《明史》前后多脱误。如天启四年三月甲寅、六月六日丙子，京师地震，《帝纪》及《五行志》俱有之。独《志》言三年京师地震者三，而《帝纪》不载；《纪》言四年三月戊午夜京师地再震，《志》亦不载。庚申夜复震者三，而《志》但云庚申再震。则其不足征明矣。

王秋泉 王秋泉者，吾邑名医也。有某富人病且死，延秋泉。秋泉适治某贵人疾，不果往。富人念不已，中夜绵惙，谓其子曰：“吾宁得一当王先生，死不恨。”子乃复走仆秋泉所，顿首敦促。会所治贵人疾良巳，又数日贵人起，治具觞秋泉，奉金币为寿。秋泉饮大醉归，归至舟中，语家人曰：“今可赴富人约矣。”而富人子所遣仆，业踊跃解维代摇橹。抵其家，传呼曰：“王先生至矣。”举家惊喜出迎。 秋泉方酣睡，家人起诸梦中。主人已盛衣冠，鞠躬入舟肃客。秋泉谢以暮夜，请得诘朝栉淋登堂。主人固请曰：“老父忍死待先生，先生幸辱临，何栉沐为？”强之入。诊脉已，与药竟出。主人盛馔揖秋泉，秋泉但摇手谢。还舟，解衣卧。鸡鸣酒醒，呼其家人骂曰：“惰奴旷乃公事！且某富人迟我久，当夜赴之，何尚泊此？”家人曰：“公顷已诊脉与药，忘之耶？”秋泉大惊曰：“审与药乎？吾真大醉，必杀之矣！”顿足，促解维归，谓不去必受辱。

家人匆遽解维，而主人已遣仆伺秋泉。闻去，即入报。须臾门启，望岸上烛笼数十，传语止王先生。秋泉不知所为。俄而主人踉跄至，入舟顿颡，泪下承睫，谢曰：“老父得先生刀圭，乃者熟寝，病若脱矣。先生存，父存；先生去，父且大去。惟先生终哀怜之。”秋泉自疑曰：“世岂有是事哉？必绐我。”然已无可奈何，强随之登堂，门且掩，心犹怦怦然。坐定，主人申谢再三：“先生用药何神验乃尔？”秋泉乃漫应曰：“昨已得其概，请更得审视。”遂入视，索药渣观之，私自慰曰：“幸不误！”更与数剂，起其疾，厚获而归。人呼为醉先生云。

外史氏曰：此事见《乌青志》。醉梦之中，而用药之神效如此，岂其中有鬼神耶？然亦可见医术之不尽足凭，而生死之自有命也！一笑。 蚺蛇 《水经注》：交趾金溪究山有大蛇，名曰蚺蛇。长十丈，围七八尺。常在树上伺鹿兽，鹿兽过，辄低头绕之。有顷，濡湿讫，便食头角。三月骨皆钻皮出。山夷始见蛇不动时，以竹签签蛇头至尾，杀而食之，以为珍异。一说以妇人衣投之，则蟠而不起走，便可得也。 杨氏南裔《异物志》曰：“蚺惟大蛇，既宏且长。采色驳荦，其文锦章。食猪吞鹿，腴成养创。宾享嘉宴，是豆是觞。”言其养创之时，肪腴甚肥，可为宾筵珍味。惜其吞食鹿冢，逢足以供老饕之大嚼也。

蚺蛇大者，能吞鹿食人。性极淫，取妇人敝袴掷地，以首戴之，俯仰顿撼甚乐。捕之者，度其出入之地，先钉罗桩数行，狭仅容其身。壮士持橄榄棍伏其中，出一人于外，飏妇人裙裤招之，蛇即昂首高六七尺来追。人退入罗桩，蛇身既巨，到狭处曲折转身不便，人持棍击之，且击且退，数人迭出，视其首俯地，则无惧矣。每击一下，则皮肉皆缩。有一泡，死而血凝（即护身胆也），其力大减。多以乱真，真者值兼金。此《岭南杂记》所言，与《水经注》合。惟《桂海虞衡志》言：蚺蛇大者如柱，常出逐鹿食之。寨兵善捕之，数辈满头插花，趋近蛇，蛇喜花，必注视，渐近俯其首。大呼“红娘子”，蛇益俯其首不动。壮士大刀断其首，众悉奔散，远伺之。有顷，蛇奋迅腾掷，道旁小木尽拔，力竭乃毙，一村饱其肉。其法更奇。然石湖所志，率经亲历，必非无据。
又按《岭表录异》云：普安州有养蛇户，每年五月五日，即舁蚺蛇入府，祗候取胆。余曾亲见，皆于大笼中藉以软草，盘屈其上。两人舁一条在地上，即以十数拐子从头翻其身，不得转折。即于腹上约其尺寸，用利刃抉之。肝胆突出，即割下其胆，曝干，以备上贡。即合内肝，以线合其疮口，收入笼。或云舁归放川泽。据诸书所称，蚺蛇力大若许，必不可以生而致。今观此录所载，则取之固自易易。其信然耶？

南裔《异物志》：蚺蛇牙长六七寸，土人尤重之，云辟不祥，利远行。卖一枚，值牛数头。 采龙眼 龙眼枝甚柔脆，熟时赁惯手登采。恐其恣啖，与约曰：“唱勿辍，辍则弗给值。”树叶扶疏，人坐绿阴中，高低断续，喁喁不已。偶听颇足娱耳，细思之，令人欲笑。 大言 少读《王莽传》，凡自法禁号令，以及名物郡县，莫不剽摹古籍，以恣粉饰，不独仿《大诰》等著作也。想见当时居之不疑，如醉如痴之状。 后读《孟蜀世家》：宋太宗遣王全斌等伐蜀，孟昶遣王昭远御之。昭远好读兵书，以方略自许。兵始发成都，昶遣李昊等饯之。昭远手执铁如意，自比诸葛亮，酒酣谓昊曰：“吾此行岂止克敌？当领此二三万雕面恶少年，取中原如反掌尔。”既而与全斌一战于三泉而败，再战于剑门而被擒。真是写成一笑！

然自古此等妄人，却又不少。南燕有王始者，莱芜人。慕容德建平四年，始以妖术惑人，众至数千，聚于太山莱芜谷。自称太平皇帝，署置百官，号其父曰太上皇，兄林为征东将军，弟泰为征西将军。帝遣车骑将军桂阳王镇讨擒之，斩于都市。临刑，人皆骂其自取族灭。或问其父兄何往，始曰：“太上皇蒙尘于外，征东、征西乱兵所害。朕躬虽存，复何聊赖？”其妻赵氏怒之曰：“君正坐此口以至此，奈何临死尚尔狂言？”始曰：“皇后何不达天命？自古岂有不亡之国，不破之家耶？”行刑者以刀环筑其口，始仰天视曰：“朕即崩矣，终不改帝号也。”此其可笑，尤堪与王莽“天生德于予，汉兵其如予何”之言并传。
又《宋稗类抄》：嘉泰开禧时，郭倪位殿岩，自谓卧龙复生，酒后辄咏“三顾频烦，两朝开济”二语。陈景俊为军漕宴之曰：“木牛流马，则以烦公。”师既溃（即富平之败），自度不复振，对客泣。时彭法传在坐，语人曰：“此带汁（字借作职）诸葛也。”

陆世科 鄞县陆世科为诸生时，尝馆于邑中一富室。值黄霉，命馆僮焙被，僮转付婢，携就主妾房中焙之。至晚，夹带主妾之睡鞋而出。世科欲睡，展被始见，抛之帐顶。 后主人入斋中见之，伺其出，袖之以去。迨更深，密令妾往扣其门，而操刀随之。世科问为谁，低应曰：“妾也。”世科曰：“焉有昏夜而女客可见先生者乎？”又令再三恳之，曰：“第开门，妾自有说。”世科曰：“女客与先生有何可说？即有说，明日与主人同来。如再不去，当即捉付尔主，勿嫌见辱也。”主人见世科毅然难犯，即应之曰：“请开门，小弟在此。”既入，世科见主人持刀，大惊。主人曰：“无惧。”出鞋示之，备述所以。世科笑曰：“幸我无私，否则已污君刃矣。”明日遂辞去。

后登万历己丑进士，仕至大理卿。是时，人多附魏阉，公独特立不阿，以完节终。 事见《乌青志》。或曰：此事已见《子不语》，彼作镇台某，不知孰是？余按《警心录》：陈淳祖为贾似道之客，守正，为诸客所恶，内侍亦恶之。一日诸姬争宠，密窃一姬鞋，藏淳祖床下，意欲并中二人也。贾入斋见之，心疑焉。夜驱此姬至斋门诱之，淳祖不应，继以大怒。贾方知其无他，勘诸姬得其情。由是深契淳祖，后有南安军之命。金、元院本演其事，与此正相类。意当时或有有意为之者，不然，或有构之者欤？据《录》中所载，则其出于依托，未可知也。

猩猩 非非子曰：“夫林密渊深，鱼鸟自有乐地。而卒为人所制者，贪其饵也。”《水经注》：“猩猩形若黄狗，又类貆豘。人面，颜容端正，音声妙丽。”楚太原王纲曰：“猩猩好酒及屐，里人置之山谷。常数辈为群，见酒物，知人张设，取之。知张者祖父姓名，詈曰：“奴欲杀我，亟舍尔去也。”即复还曰：“姑尝酒。”迨醉，取屐著之，卒为人擒焉。”放翁诗：“已醉猩猩犹著屐，入秋燕燕尚营巢。”此物爱酒与屐，他书亦言之厉历，当不虚也。 按唐人小说载：安南武平州封溪中，有猩猩焉。如美人，解人语，知往事。以嗜酒故，以屐得之。槛百数同牢，欲食之，众自推肥者，相送流涕。时饷封溪令，以帕盖之，令问何物，猩猩笼中语曰：“惟有仆并酒一壶耳！”令笑而受之。盖此物之灵慧如是，其胜于陆机之黄耳传书多矣。而卒以爱酒与屐，为人所制。《礼记》：“猩猩能言，不离禽兽。”信夫！然岂独禽兽已哉？

燕妒 广陵牛氏家，堂燕方育雏。其雌为猫所毙，雄啁哳久之，翻然而逝。少选偕一雌来，共哺其子。明日有雏堕地，至晚诸雏毕死。取视之，满吭皆枲耳，实盖为雌所毒也。嗟乎！禽鸟犹疾其前雏如此，而雄不悟，悲夫！ 去年仲夏，沈荔堂家远香书舍中，燕已育雏，一雌为蛇所噬。越宿，其雄偕—雌至，相与哺雏。未几蛇又至，时雄出未返，雌惊起，啄其目。蛇甫吞一雏入口，不能反噬，急吐出，蜿蜒遁去。雏已垂毙，雌覆而翼之，间衔庭中旱莲草哺之，未几遂愈。然自此雌偶出必速返，朝夕不离于侧，蛇亦绝不复至。是此雌又能为诸雏之义鹘也。 戒贪 《金楼子》：齐桓公卧于柏寝。白鸟营营，饥而求饱。公开翠纱之厨而进焉。有知礼者，不食而退；有知足者，隽肉而退；有不知足者，长吁短吸而食。及其饱也，腹为之溃。盖戒夫贪也。

余尝见蚊有腹已果而作红色者，其尾血滴不止，而吸食犹未已也。驱之，则栖于屏案间，不能复飞。斯时不知亦悔其饕餮太过否？ 师戒 里中有走无常者，尝一卧数日。一日乍醒，遽问其家人曰：“吾里外科岑氏子，昨已死乎？”家人曰：“然。君至冥中亦见之乎？”曰：“吾昨于岳庙城隍庑下，见鬼卒拘岑至。城隍拍案怒曰：‘汝在阳间做得好事！’岑叩首涕泣曰：‘小人生前并未敢造恶。’城隍怒曰：‘观尔獐头鼠脸，胸中岂有一点墨？奈何既以牛医杀人，更托名教书诳钱财而误人子弟乎？’命鬼卒拽下予杖。岑复叩首曰：‘小人虽托名世医，然从无过而问津者，势不得草菅人命。第为饥寒所迫，权行训蒙度日。身分生平所读，止有一部《四书》，又大半句读不全，故所取修金，极丰不过二两。大约不过菜佣舆卒、目不识丁者之子弟，愿相从受业。彼亦只图省费，无意深求。若《四书》以上，小人亦不敢妄教，故犹不致大误。’城隍色少纾。

“顾判官取册检视，至岑首一行，注曰：’绵蛮（读作变）黄鸟。‘城隍怒曰：‘此辈只合转入畜生道中耳。’又检至下一行，注云：‘如恶恶（皆读作屋）臭，如好好（皆读上声）色。’城隍笑曰：‘二字如此读，试问作何解？’岑曰：‘此当读为四句，言如其为恶，须如恶臭，斯为真恶；如为好人，须如美人，斯为真好。则善恶之意皆诚矣。’城隍曰：‘然则后文恶而知其美者，又作何解？’岑曰：‘此恶字当读去声。盖恶之为物，天下未有以为美者。但据《本草》，则人中黄之益人多矣。是其味美于回也。故孟子日：恶（句）在其敬叔父也。’

“城隍骂曰：‘畜类！汝平日以此教人，尚谓未尝误人耶？’遂命罚作狗，恣其食恶以偿之。岑复叩首曰：‘小人生前以饮啖兼人，中多痰火，每当暑喘作，其苦万状。愿大王垂谅，罚作一牛。’城隍讶问曰：‘此又何说？’对曰：‘小人向读《千家诗》有云：赤日行天牛不知。惟牛能不受暑热也。’城隍大笑，令鬼卒拽下，先杖一百，仍押回里中，俾投生为牛，为课徒者示警云。” 家人皆未信。次日，闻比邻畜牛生犊，往觇之，果然。戏呼其名，犊辄昂首掉尾而鸣，若应声然。 牡丹 《日知录》：山东人刻《金石录》，于李易安《后序》“绍兴二年玄黓岁壮月朔”，不知“壮月”之出于《尔雅》，而改为“牡丹”。凡历代以来所刻之书，皆“牡丹”之类也。

又《拊掌录》：绍兴九年，金归我河南地。商贾携长安秦汉碑刻，求售于士大夫。王锡老得一碑，无一字可辨，王独称赏不已。客曰：“此何代碑乎？”王不能答。客曰：“我知之，是名没字碑。”一笑而散。今之赏鉴家，大率皆没字碑之类也。 柳画 乾隆辛丑十月，萧山陆敬轩为永城尉。署中旧有柳树一枝，年久半槁。命工伐之，其中纹画如淡墨写成：左右峰石峻削，悬崖之上有松一株，藤缠累累；老树一株，枝叶皆倒垂。下有一叟，挟杖立，高冠长袖，须眉宛然。其左手纳袖中，著胸前，右脚前行露其舄，左脚隐衣下，回顾若听泉状。虽妙手写生，不是过也。从来木理之成文者，有影木之类，乃得之柳树中，则又闻所未闻也。造物之巧，岂可思哉！ 又康熙壬寅，京口檄造战舰。江都刘氏园中，有银杏一株，百余年物也，亦被伐。及锯开，则木之立理有观音大士像二，妙曼天然。众共骇异，乃施之持南福缘庵中。此似有神物凭之者，尤奇。

闽人吴玉长璧，尝适杭。适金中丞家招宴，庖人烹圆鱼。既熟，剖之，一肉观音，头戴金冠，盛妆饰，眉目衣褶皆如画，右手下垂，左手中按，足踏芙蓉一朵。座客无不惊惋，遂命覆羹。 此事见樊榭《城东杂记》。岂真大士现身，以为杀身之戒者欤？其他如《酉阳杂俎》载：炀帝食蛤，中有一佛二菩萨像。唐文宗时，鳖中有观音大士像。《续夷坚志》载：史浩食蛤，中有二佛像，螺髻璎珞，足踏莲花。《异识资谐》载：邵崇益剖蚌，中有罗汉像。隽区言：双林镇民剖蚌，中有珍珠八仙。《夷坚丙志》载：郑伯膺于楚州蚌中得观音像，妙相端严，杨枝净瓶备具。又于蟹腹内得鬼判，毛发森立，怪恶可畏。《坚觚集》言：遂昌县民剖鳖中，有比丘端坐，握牟尼珠，衣履斩然。唐询家鸡卵中，有菩萨坐莲花。凡此犹曰仙佛现身，以示杀生之戒。

至如他书所载，蚕茧中有小佛像，状如入定观音。鸡卵中有猕猴。如此类，则又何说？盖妖异之兴，终非常情所可揣测也。 湖市 嘉庆庚午四月，高邮县西门临湖石堤倾圮，河帅委员修筑。有州署幕友夏友香者，督工役往来堤上。日将暮矣，忽见湖中城市宛然，林木繁茂，断岸一带小桥亘之，桥旁有斥堠列栅与拒马咸备，桥上有人持板伞作迎风急走势，而柳阴之下，二驴啮草于其间。惟时落日沉山，暮霞四起，适当湖中城门阙处，金碧万道。沿堤水纹如縠，与夕阳相激宕，光怪陆离，不可名状。

城中炊烟缕缕，杰阁嵯峨，浮屠高耸，钟声如在耳也。晚风乍起，而所谓城市林木桥亭楼阁者，渐淡渐远，顷刻尽灭，然已逾数刻矣。尝闻山东登、莱有海市，四川青城、钱城有山市，今此处更有湖市，亦奇观也。 冰山录 分宜籍没，有为《冰山录》以纪其事者。王仁裕《开元天宝遗事》：杨国忠权倾天下，进取之士争诣其门。进士张彖者，有大名。有劝彖修谒国忠，可图荣显。彖笑曰：“汝辈以为杨公之势，倚靠如泰山。以吾所见，乃冰山也。或皎日大明之际，则此山当误人尔。”后果如其言，人以张生见几。后年，生及第，释褐华阴县尉。时令守皆非其人，张生有吏道，每有申举，守令辄抑而不从。生慨然曰：“大丈夫有凌霄盖世之志，而拘于下位，若立身矮屋中，使人抬头不得。”遂拂衣归，遁于嵩山。《录》盖本此义。

泰山 或言泰山没字碑非碑也，度其中必有所藏，当是封禅碑铭及玉版检金泥之属。昔有一巡方恶其疑众，命撤之。甫动其盖，风雷骤作。说似近怪，然其中有物无疑。顾宁人则谓《史》、《汉》但言立石，而不言刻石。足见读书心细。然《隋书·经籍志》有《秦皇东巡会稽刻石文》一卷，当即指所立之石。是此言亦未可据为定论也。 夷俗 《高丽图经》言：其俗往往男女同川而浴。而西南苗夷跳月之法，必先野合生子，而后成婚。以为夷俗之难以廉耻喻也。顾其间亦自有所谓廉耻者： 粤西瑶僮山居者，妇人四月即入水浴，至九月方止。男女时亦相杂，或触其私，不忌；惟触其乳，则怒相击杀，以为此乃妇道所分，故极重之。此一种节义也。

暹罗之俗：遇华人与其妇通者，则其夫皆喜，以为荣。或邀之共饮，谓其妻美，故华人爱之也。此一种见识也。（又闻暹罗男阳皆镶嵌镜铃珠玉，富贵金银，贫用铜锡，行则琅琅有声。婚娶：群僧迎送，婿至女家，僧取女红帖男额，谓之利市。） 台湾土番，其人不知历日，无祖先祭祀。自父母外，无伯叔甥舅之称。重生女，不重生男。不论有无生育，往往互相交易。暑月男女皆裸体对坐。淫欲之事，长则避幼，若弟妹子女，略不羞避。此又一种分别也。 若夫乌浒之人，娶妻而美，则让其兄。罗鬼之卒，新妇见舅姑不拜，裸体而进盥，谓之奉堂。则居然习于礼让也矣。 西洋文郎马神，其俗不淫奸者论死。惟华人与夷女通，则削其发，即以女妻之，不听归也。昔有人杀其夫者，其妻控诸邑。邑令怒，即以其妻妻之，曰：“使汝妻亦守寡。”其断狱之法，盖有所受之也。

双林凌氏 常熟沈孝子墓志：鼎革时，尝负母而行于野。遇盗夺其糒，母不与，盗怒，将杀之。孝子泣而求代，并舍之。邻失火，延母寝。母病方剧，不可以变。孝子号痛呼天，反风火息。后母年八十余遘危疾，医者皆曰法不可治。刲股以进，弗瘥。梦神绯衣告曰：“疾非五药所治。医凌某，在双林。”亟致之。凌至，以针达之，脱然愈。见《望溪集》。言孝子之至行，足以格盗而感神也。而凌氏之以针灸名其家也，岂偶然哉！凌氏子孙，盖世其业至今云。 孝子名育，卒于康熙四十九年，年九十四，雍正间翰林编修淑之祖也。 附录《碧里志存》 凌汉章，湖州人。成化间针灸神灵，擅名吴浙。《两浙名贤录》称其慷慨负义气，见人之病，如痛在身。有延之者，昏夜风雨，无不疾赴。砭石所投，诸恙脱然。每辰起门启，舆疾求治者日数十百人。贫者未尝受直，故身死之日，家无余资。

杨园先生 杨园先生葬其亲，既卜兆，而村民阻葬，弗克。因厝柩于庄，命佃户居守。盗至，纵火焚其庄，灾及两柩。及罪人既得，斩首祭墓，而先生衵衣用粗麻终其身。 婿尤介锡，幼而能文，负笈从游，言规行矩，甚相得也。先生以女妻之。及其兄师锡举进士，耽酒色，介锡背先生而效焉。屡训弗悛，其后买娼为妾。先生女素娴闺训，引诗书以讽谏。婿以其逆耳，与妾谋，鸩杀之。 先生往哭，见其被鸩状，讼之官。褫其衿，逐其妾，卒未正其杀妻之罪也。而先生自子死后，其孤孙亦相继夭殂，后嗣绝矣。 外史氏曰：陈古铭先生曰梓，年二十，侍姚蛰庵先生。先生为言：“下愚不移，如尤婿玷杨园。而周婿又玷诚庵，其执柯者杨园也。此亦先师痛心事。然天下固有不可化诲之人，一杀妻，一为盗，于两先生何病哉！”此论固然。然如杨园先生之所遭，何其酷耶！余尝与梦庐论之，梦庐曰：“是则所谓命也。”呜呼！其信然耶？

按先生年谱，崇祯七年甲戌，先生年二十四，馆颜士凤家。时东南文社方兴，先生与士凤相约毋滥赴，但与同学数子邱衡辈，文行相切磋而已。然先生自与严颖生、邱季心、凌渝安诸君子游，往往以举业为戒。或有延课子弟者，相率辞不赴，以其为功利起见也。年六十，姚公玉瑚偕其弟琏，谒先生于张佩葱斋中。适语溪以《东皋遗选》数十册，托佩葱发出。舟子负上，连呼重甚。先生戏语曰：“此未必重，吾以为轻如鸿毛耳。”姚因问：“学问之于举业，固可并行而无妨耶？抑必屏去而后可从事耶？”先生正色曰：“《诗》有之：荼蓼朽止，黍稷茂止。”盖其持己之严如此。
又先生与吴裒仲书曰：“天与仁孝，知有勿药之喜。读终天一记，辄为泫然不已，真与‘蓼蓼者莪’同一哀切也。人子至此，盖已无可如何，惟有临渊履冰，如曾子之志而已。《记》曰：‘终为难。’而申之曰：‘终也者，非终父母之身，终其身也。’然则人子未死一日，是亦事亲之一日也。愿与仁兄终勉之耳。”

水月庵 武林艮山门外水月庵，即水月老人故居。老人姓孙名文，字文石，号水月。会稽诸生。国变后，隐于杭，榜所居曰梅园。性恬静，一介不取，间为长短歌词。问其年，尝称九十。发尽秃，人多以僧呼之。 沈阳范忠贞公幼时，老人尝抚其顶曰：“是儿当建节吾土，吾犹及见之。”及忠贞抚浙，太夫人言于忠贞，物色得之，屏驺从往谒，谈论数四。时西溪多虎，公告之故。老人曰：“山头大虫任打，门内大虫怕惹。”及忠贞任闽督，老人送之曰：“耳后火发时，须要有主意。”后忠贞竟死耿难。人始悟其前知，争就之。老人厌恶避去，不知所终。土人思之，改其居为水月庵。肖老人若僧像奉之，为香火院。《池北偶谈》称为水月和尚。

外史氏曰：《熙朝新语》亦尝载此事，而不及“耳后火发”两语，并不详其生平为明季遗老也。夫事由前定，老人已知之矣，而卒不肯屈节于新朝，亦犹龚诩之不仕成祖，谓恐负金川门一恸耳。古来忠臣孝子，岂肯以时命之故移其志哉！ 腹语 《聊斋志异》言：有—盗被刑，数武之外，犹旋转而呼曰：“好快刀！”此只极形刀之快耳。尝举以语人，而人皆笑之。按《明史·杨维斗传》：国变后，先生临刑不屈，首已坠，而声从项出，则《聊斋》之说非诬也。又汉贾雍为豫章太守，与敌人战，丧元，犹带弓擐甲，挟马归营，问众将曰：“有头佳乎？无头佳乎？”众将曰：“有头佳。”雍腹语曰：“无头亦佳。”凡此亦如醉者坠车不死，其神全也。 尝闻唐花卿为剑南节度使，命讨段子璋，平之。其后出师，以单骑出战，陷入重围，丧其首，犹临溪沃盥。有浣女见之曰：“无头何以洗盥？”闻言遂仆。此神散之验也。

又：太原王穆，至德初，为鲁旻部将。于南阳战败，贼骑自后追及，以剑斫穆颈，筋骨俱断，惟喉尚连。初不自觉死，食顷方悟。而头在脐上，旋觉食漏，遂以手力扶，还附颈。须臾复落，闷绝如初，久之方苏。正颈之后，以发分系两畔，乃能起坐。而所骑马，初不离穆；穆方一足践镫，而左缚发解，头坠怀中。夜复苏，复系发正首，心念马卧方可得上，马忽横伏，穆因得上马。马亦随起，载穆东南行。穆两手捧两颊，行四十里。穆麾下散卒数十人，群行见之，扶寄村舍，寻载适军。穆于城中养病二百余日方愈。绕颈有肉如指，头竟小偏。此则头落数次而可续也，尤奇。 嘉庆初，苗匪煽乱楚蜀。官兵讨之，战于香炉坪，贼败。有贼目为官兵所杀，头已落，犹手持大斧作迎敌状，颈中白气一缕冲起，径二丈许，逾时乃仆。此或有邪术焉，又非寻常所可同语矣。

刘子壮 刘子壮，字克猷，湖北黄冈人。少颖慧，读书一目数行。属文奇肆，中崇祯庚午举人。领荐后，梦神告之曰：“尔须朱之弼作房官，方中春榜。”及至京，偶出寓散步，见数童子携书包经其门。一童子特秀出，执手与谈，见其书上写学名朱之弼，大惊。随至其家。其父乃开柴厂者，赠笔砚数事，珍重而别。后遭流寇之乱，不赴春官。及本朝顺治己丑会试，朱之弼已为分校，得首卷，即刘也。 亦见《熙朝新语》。读此知穷达有命，迟早亦有定数，为之慨然。 熊伯龙 先时廷对策，俱用四六。顺治己丑科，世祖临轩策士，命勿用四六旧套。刘子壮对策称旨，亲定一甲第一名，与榜眼熊伯龙齐名。熊典试浙江，一榜得三状元：乙未史大成，甲辰严我斯，庚戌蔡启傅。士林荣之。

库中画 明大内有画藏库中，累朝不开。至崇祯时，上欲开视，珰以从来未开为言。而上意甚坚，珰不敢逆，遂开。进，无所有，惟后小红箱一只。捧至，上书崇桢某年某月日开。上以其预定也，益异之。及启视，止盛画三轴：其一作无数军民相反背，其一则无数官吏士民仓皇逃窜之状。上曰：“嘻！乱离不远矣。”其三则止有一人披发赤体，悬于树上，其貌则俨然御容也。群珰动容，上怃然不乐而出。 乩书 崇祯时，宫中每年或召仙，或召将，叩以来岁事，无弗应者。以前一召即至，至是久之不至。良久，武帝下临，乩批云：“天将俱已降生人间，无可应召者。”上再拜，叩以天将降生，意欲何为？尚有未生者否？批云：“惟汉寿亭侯受明深思，不忍下降。”批毕寂然，再叩不应矣。

玉人 鹤民国人长三寸，日行八千里，其疾如飞。每为海鹤所食。其人性极机巧，乃刻玉为己状，鼓百成群，聚子荒野水次。鹤以为小人也，吞之而死。后他鹤见真者，反不敢食。 今世之傅虎以翼而食人者多矣，然其中岂无玉人焉？惟食之者之智不如鹤，故往往饕餮相踵而不悟，不免为小人所误耳。 天主教 天主名耶稣，大西洋人。自万历间，有大西洋意利亚人利玛窦，航海九万里，抵广东之香山澳，始传其教于中土。其言谓耶稣生子如德亚，其国在亚细亚洲之中，一名拂菻，即古大秦国。于时为汉哀帝元寿二年，历一千五百八十一年，至万历九年卒。其国自开辟以来，六千余年矣，天主乃肇生人类之邦。帝嘉其远来，给赐优厚，遂于京师宣武门内建天主堂。藻绘诡异，供耶稣像。右圣母，貌若少女，手一儿，耶稣也。于是其教大行。

相传愿入救者，其师令服清水一盂，心遂迷惑，乃予之白金五十两，故投者甚众。其归也，必令家人毁祖先牌位、灶神、门神之属，而专奉十字木架。遇物作十字形者，即不敢亵。若奉教者物故，其师辄遣两人至尸傍唪经，以布掩尸，视验讫，始去。或以为窃取两目瞳子故也。 尝有人贫甚，称贷无所。稔知入其教者，可得五十金，乃预戒家人，俟得银而归，灌以药物使吐。后其人受教归，果欲毁神牌，奉十字架。家人絷其手足，以药灌之，始吐清水，最后涌出一血团乃止。其家人将血团贮于水盆，经宿血散，中有一物不散，乃成人形，须眉毕具。细视其状，即授教师也。

大胆 《吴应箕传》：南都不守，起兵应金声。败走山中，被获，慷慨就死。时有吴汉超者，亦起兵与邱祖德、麻三衡诸军相合，连取句容、溧水，高淳、溧阳、泾、太平诸县。明年正月，袭南城宁国，夜缘南城登，兵溃。城中按首事者，汉超已出城，念母在，且恐累人，入见曰：“首事者我也。”剖其胆，长三寸。如是则姜维之胆如鸡卵，又不足言矣。 项王走马埒 《石柱记》：弁山旧有项王饮马池、系马石、走马埒。朱少师《考异》云：项羽避难吴中，过大溪。有异物。早暮以尾卷人畜食之。羽跨其背，一手扼颈，两足夹其腹，一手抱树，连拔大树数章。天曙视之，马也。遍体黑龙纹，遂以名溪，今之龙溪是也。明郑明选诗：“项王昔走马，四面黄金埒。时衰骓不逝，悲歌对红颊。”

埋忧续集卷二 无支祈 《古岳渎经》：禹治水三至桐柏，惊风迅雷。禹怒，召百灵，命应龙搜逐之。乃获淮涡水神，名无支祈，形若猿猴，缩额高鼻，青躯白首，金目雪牙，伸颈百尺，力逾九象，搏击腾疾，倏忽不可久视。禹授之童律，不能制；授之乌术田，不能制；授之庚辰，庚辰持戟追获。颈锁大索，鼻穿金铃，徙之淮阳之龟山足下。《山海经》云：水兽好为雷雨，禹锁之君山足下，其名巫支祈。即其物也。 唐时有御史欲见此孽，出罪人遍摸其所，抓得之。用牛六十四头，以盘车拽锁出之。锁将尽，怪跃空中，大呼一声如霹雳，锁连人牛俱没。
